EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from the gulf coast to offshore the Carolinas and up the east coast from the 18th to the 19th. This storm produced heavy snow across Garrett county Maryland, portions of northern West Virginia, and portions of Fayette,  Westmorland, and Greene counties in Pennsylvania. Total storm accumulations were from one to 2 feet in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia with over a foot in Garrett county Maryland. Storm totals were 6 to 12 inches elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving across the Great Lakes brought of mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the ridges of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Ice accumulated from 1/4 to 1/2 inch across the region the night of December 8th into the morning of December 9th before the precipitation changed to all rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As warm air headed north ahead of low pressure moving into the Ohio Valley freezing rain quickly developed across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Untreated roadways quickly accumulated up to one tenth of an inch of ice making travel nearly impossible between 6 AM and 10 AM. Major interstates in and around Pittsburgh, including the Pennsylvania Turnpike were closed for more than 2 hours until roads could be treated and surface temperatures warmed above freezing. 911 centers across the area received thousands of call due to minor accidents. 2 more serious accidents resulted in 2 deaths, one in Beaver county, PA and one in Westmoreland county, PA.

A strong pressure gradient behind a cold front produced damaging winds across all of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. Sustained winds were from 30 to 40 MPH with gusts over 60 MPH. The strongest reported wind gusts were 68 MPH in Franklin, PA and 66 MPH in Eighty Four, PA. Trees and power lines were reported down across the region, with more than 50,000 homes without power late afternoon on the 9th at the peak of the winds.

A strong area of low pressure moved north across West Virginia to western Pennsylvania producing a heavy mix of precipitation. Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across northwest Pennsylvania on the night of the 27Th before changing to freezing rain the morning of the 28Th, then back to snow in the afternoon with another 1 to 2 inches. Ice accumulations in Pittsburgh and areas north were from a quarter to one half inch. For southeast Ohio, northern West Virgina, and Garrett county Maryland mixed precipitation changed to freezing rain during the evening of the 27Th with ice quickly accumulating to one half inch before changing to plain overnight. The rain changed back to snow during the afternoon of the 28Th with 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulating.

A slow moving cold front triggered numerous thunderstorms across south central Pennsylvania during the early to mid afternoon. The convective environment was characterized by moderate instability and relatively weak vertical wind shear, resulting in slow moving storms that produced multiple reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Waynesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north across West Virginia to western Pennsylvania producing a heavy mix of precipitation. Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across northwest Pennsylvania on the night of the 27Th before changing to freezing rain the morning of the 28Th, then back to snow in the afternoon with another 1 to 2 inches. Ice accumulations in Pittsburgh and areas north were from a quarter to one half inch. For southeast Ohio, northern West Virgina, and Garrett county Maryland mixed precipitation changed to freezing rain during the evening of the 27Th with ice quickly accumulating to one half inch before changing to plain overnight. The rain changed back to snow during the afternoon of the 28Th with 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulating.

A strong area of arctic high pressure moved from the Northern Plains on on January 15th to the Upper Ohio Valley by late in the day on January 16th.  The high eventually shifted east of the area on the 17th.  Very cold temperatures accompanied this high.  The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were recorded at most observing sites.  Gusty southwest to west winds made it feel even colder with wind chills colder than minus 25 reported from the evening of the 15th through the afternoon hours of the 16th.  Winds eventually lessened early on the 17th which allowed temperatures to again plummet to 10 below or colder for the third morning in a row at many locations.  Most of the schools in northwestern Pennsylvania were closed on both the 15th and 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures were reported in Crawford County on January 15th through the 17th.  Temperatures were the coldest since January of 1994.  Gusty southwest to west winds accompanied the cold temperatures and created wind chills colder than minus 25 for several hours on January 16th.  Lows at the Meadville Airport on the 15th through 17th were: 6, -11 and -8.  A cooperative observer near Titusville reported morning lows from the 15th through 17th of -7, -5 and -16.  At Springboro, mornings lows from the 15th through 17th were: -6, -12 and -11.  Near Jamestown, mornings lows from the 15th through 17th were: -5, -11 and -14.  Finally, lows at Linesville on the 15th through 17th were: -6, -11 and -11.  Most if not all of the schools in the county were closed on the 15th and 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie on January 18th.  A cold front extending south from the low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania just before daybreak on the 18th.  Cold westerly winds behind the front caused lake effect snow bands to quickly develop.  The snow showers tapered off during the evening hours after dumping 6 to 10 inches of snow on much of Erie and Crawford Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Erie County just before daybreak on January 18th.  Cold westerly winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to quickly develop.  The snow showers tapered off during the early morning hours of January 19th after dumping 6 to 10 inches of snow on most of northern Erie County.  A maximum of 10.1 inches was measured in Mill Creek Township.  Officially, 5.4 inches of snow was measured at Erie International Airport on the 18th.  A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southeast across the Great Lakes triggered severe thunderstorms across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours. Several of these storms moved repeatedly across the same areas of Columbia County, causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms produced heavy rain over portions of Columbia County. Two rain gauge reports of 3.55 inches and 4.00 inches was recorded over several hours just east of the Bloomsburg fairgrounds. Overall, there were about 15 roads closed throughout the county including five in Hemlock Township, four in Scott Township, three in the city of Bloomsburg, two in Franklin Township, and at one in each of Montour, Catawissa and Locust Townships. ||Three rock/mud slides were also reported from the heavy rain, in Franklin Township,  Scott Township and near the Bloomsburg Fairgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley pushed warm and humid air northward ahead of a cold front moving east with the surface low. Severe thunderstorms were widespread across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Significant flash flooding occurred over eastern Allegheny and western Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania with estimated damage of 18 million dollars to public infrastructure and private buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an estimated wind gust to 60 MPH on Mount Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley pushed warm and humid air northward ahead of a cold front moving east with the surface low. Severe thunderstorms were widespread across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Significant flash flooding occurred over eastern Allegheny and western Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania with estimated damage of 18 million dollars to public infrastructure and private buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported widespread flash flooding across much of western amd central Westmorland county from Murrysville to Greensburg and Mount Pleasant. At least 1,192 buildings were affected, with 136 having major damage and 11 buildings destroyed. Damages were estimated to be around 9 million dollars. Most major roadways had flooding and some were closed well after the water receded to clear debris. The hardest hit areas from Murrysville to Greensburg had rainfall of over 4 inches in 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent lake effect snow showers produced three to six inches of snowfall across the Northwest Mountains, with localized amounts near one foot across the snow belt region in the northwest part of Warren county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports indicated a general three to six inch snow accumulation across Warren County by the evening of the 8th, with localized storm totals around ten inches by the morning of the 9th. The heaviest snows fell across the snow belt region in northwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent lake effect snow showers produced three to six inches of snowfall across the Northwest Mountains, with localized amounts near one foot across the snow belt region in the northwest part of Warren county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports indicated a general two to four inch snow accumulation across McKean County by the evening of the 8th, with storm totals between six to seven inches by the morning of the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain, with some snow at the start, began falling during the late evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th across Berks County and Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs. The rain fell heavy at times between 5 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged around two inches. The concentration of the heavy rain and some headwater snow melt in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the streams and creeks in the southeastern part of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and creek flooding in Delaware County. The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 4 p.m. EST through 1108 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 9.76 feet at 815 p.m. EST. The Chester Creek in Chester was above its 8 foot flood stage from 754 a.m. EST through 1231 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 9.72 feet at 945 a.m. EST. Crum Creek at Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 727 a.m. EST through 1248 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 7.38 feet at 930 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 2.13 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered showers and thunderstorms produced damaging winds in eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 3rd. This convection formed right along a cold front and was able to mix the stronger winds aloft to the ground as they moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph was measured at the Lehigh Valley International Airport as a line of showers and isolated thunderstorms moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A steady rain that fell from the afternoon of the 2nd into the early morning of the 3rd preceding a cold front produced isolated creek flooding across Eastern Pennsylvania. Event precipitation totals average around an inch and a half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain caused minor flooding along sections of the main stem of the Perkiomen Creek. The Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 528 a.m. EST through 952 a.m. EST on the 3rd. It crested at 4.45 feet at 730 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.56 inches in Gilbertsville, 1.44 inches in Collegeville and 1.41 inches in Pottstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred across most of Eastern Pennsylvania from the morning into the afternoon of the 13th. Ice accretions averaged between one and two tenths and led to many accidents on untreated roadways, bridges and overpasses. Freezing rain began between 8 a.m. EST (in the south) and 10 a.m. EST in the Poconos. As warmer air moved in at the surface, the freezing rain changed to rain by Noon EST in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs and Berks County and during the first half of the afternoon in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The rain ended around 6 p.m. EST.||In Monroe County, parts of Interstates 80 and 380 and Pennsylvania State Route 115 were closed. In Northampton County, numerous accidents were reported on Interstate 78 and U.S. Route 22.| |The freezing rain resulted from precipitation that formed ahead of an advancing surface warm front. While it was warm enough aloft for rain, surface temperatures lagged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow was reported across the central and southern half of Cambria County. Lesser amounts ranging from two to four inches were common over the northern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper-like system brought moderate to heavy snowfall to north central Pennsylvania. The snow started early in the morning and tapered off in the evening. Snow accumulations generally ranged from four to eight inches followed by a brief wintry mixture of sleet, freezing rain and freezing drizzle which resulted in a light glazing of ice. ||Further south across the Laurel Highlands, warmer air caused snow and sleet to change to freezing rain for most of the event. This resulted in a significant ice accumulation of one quarter inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow was reported across McKean County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong arctic air mass brought sub-freezing temperatures and extreme wind chills to much of central Pennsylvania. This was one of the coldest air masses to affect the region in years. Overnight low temperatures fell to almost thirty below zero in some locations, while highs struggled to climb out of the single digits above and below zero. The cold outbreak closed or delayed many schools over the two to three day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures and brisk winds produced dangerous wind chills of twenty to thirty degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong arctic air mass brought sub-freezing temperatures and extreme wind chills to much of central Pennsylvania. This was one of the coldest air masses to affect the region in years. Overnight low temperatures fell to almost thirty below zero in some locations, while highs struggled to climb out of the single digits above and below zero. The cold outbreak closed or delayed many schools over the two to three day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures and brisk winds produced wind chills of fifteen to twenty five degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred across most of Eastern Pennsylvania from the morning into the afternoon of the 13th. Ice accretions averaged between one and two tenths and led to many accidents on untreated roadways, bridges and overpasses. Freezing rain began between 8 a.m. EST (in the south) and 10 a.m. EST in the Poconos. As warmer air moved in at the surface, the freezing rain changed to rain by Noon EST in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs and Berks County and during the first half of the afternoon in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The rain ended around 6 p.m. EST.||In Monroe County, parts of Interstates 80 and 380 and Pennsylvania State Route 115 were closed. In Northampton County, numerous accidents were reported on Interstate 78 and U.S. Route 22.| |The freezing rain resulted from precipitation that formed ahead of an advancing surface warm front. While it was warm enough aloft for rain, surface temperatures lagged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and isolated thunderstorms that preceded a cold front caused heavy rain to fall across northwestern Chester County overnight on the 19th. The runoff caused flooding in that part of the county. Rain began falling during the evening of the 19th, but fell at its heaviest just before the cold frontal passage between 1 a.m. EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 20th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached three inches in extreme northwestern Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain in northwestern Chester County caused flooding along sections of the West Branch of the Brandywine Creek. The West Branch of the Brandywine Creek near Honey Brook was above its 7 foot flood stage from 529 a.m. EST through 746 a.m. EST on the 20th. It crested at 7.44 feet at 630 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.52 inches in Morgantown (Berks County) and 1.24 inches in Honey Brook (Chester County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the region on December 9th.  Cold westerly winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop across northwestern Pennsylvania just before daybreak on the 10th.  Light to moderate snow was reported through the morning hours of the 10th with a lull in activity early in the afternoon.  By sunset, moderate to heavy snow was again falling.  Heavy snow continued on and off through the night with light snow reported during the daylight hours of the 11th.  This activity finally tapered off during the evening of the 11th.  Westerly winds gusted to as much as 30 mph much of this event.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Some locations in Erie County saw more than a foot of snow during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold westerly winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop before daybreak on December 10th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported in Erie County during the morning hours of the 10th.  After a brief lull in the activity during the middle part of the day, the lake effect activity picked up again by early evening.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then fell through daybreak on the 11th.  Light snow continued through the daylight hours of the 11th before finally tapering off.  Visibilities during the periods of moderate to heavy snow were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of greater than an inch per hour.  Snowfall totals across northern Erie County generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches.  A peak total of 19.0 inches was reported southwest of North East.  Officially, 8.1 inches of snow was measured at Erie International Airport on the 10th and 11th.  Westerly winds gusted to as much as 30 mph for much of this event.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting which hampered travel.   Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the central Great Lakes on December 9th. Strong southeast winds developed ahead of this system during the early morning hours of the 9th.  Winds gusted to as much as 60 mph along and near the south shore of Lake Erie beginning around 430 am.  Then, a strong cold front stretching south from the low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of the 9th.  Showers developed ahead of the front and moved across Erie and Crawford Counties early in the afternoon.  Brief wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported with a few of the stronger showers.  Damaging southwest winds also occurred behind this cold front.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Crawford County during the afternoon hours of December 9th.  A cluster of showers developed in advance of this front and moved across the county early in the afternoon.  Brief wind gusts in excess of 55 mph were reported with the showers.  Southwest winds behind the front rapidly increased during the afternoon hours with peak gusts of around 60 mph reported late in the day.  Winds continued to gust to as much as 50 mph through 10 pm.  The high winds downed trees, utility poles and power lines in Crawford County.  Scattered power outages were reported.  A few homes and buildings in the county sustained minor damage.  Most of this was from lost siding or roofing.  Some of this damage was associated with the showers that moved through early in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked eastward across the northern Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of the 20th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front and tracked east across the area. Many of the thunderstorms became severe in the very warm and humid airmass in place across the region during the late afternoon and evening hours. The thunderstorms diminished and moved east of the area around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported in Erie County.  Peak wind gusts were estimated to be around 45 mph.  A few trees and power lines were downed resulting in scattered power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and leftover moisture in the area gave the region a second round of wintry weather during the evening of the 31st and the early morning of January 1st. Snow mainly fell in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos where additional accumulations were an inch or less. In Berks County and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, a wintry mix of mainly sleet and freezing rain fell. Ice accretions were less than one tenth of an inch, but any untreated walkways and roadways were hazardous. The precipitation spread from south to north between 6 p.m. and 8 p.m. EST on the 31st and ended between 1 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST on January 1st.

A strong pressure gradient behind a cold front produced damaging winds across all of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. Sustained winds were from 30 to 40 MPH with gusts over 60 MPH. The strongest reported wind gusts were 68 MPH in Franklin, PA and 66 MPH in Eighty Four, PA. Trees and power lines were reported down across the region, with more than 50,000 homes without power late afternoon on the 9th at the peak of the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Coast Guard reported wind driven high waves on the Ohio River caused the towboat Sam S to sink to the bottom of the river near Georgetown in Beaver county.|The crew of the towboat was rescued by another boat with no injuries.

In Berks County and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, a wintry mix of mainly sleet and freezing rain fell. Ice accretions were less than one tenth of an inch, but any untreated walkways and roadways were hazardous. The precipitation spread from south to north between 6 p.m. and 8 p.m. EST on the 31st and ended between 1 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST on January 1st.

Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage late in the afternoon across extreme southeast Pennsylvania.  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and coupled with the heavy rain, helped knock down shallow rooted trees, weak tree limbs and wires. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed throughout Eastern Pennsylvania, but peak wind gusts were on average between 40 and 45 mph.  A few additional weak tree limbs and poles were knocked down. The peak wind gust at the Philadelphia International Airport on the 9th was 50 mph, 10th 44 mph and 11th 40 mph.

The combination of the strong northwest winds during the day on the 29th and the larger tide ranges associated with the approaching full moon spring tides produced blowout tides during the early evening low tide on the 29th. In Philadelphia, the low tide reached 2.54 feet below mean lower low water. Low water problems start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported a few miles southeast of Dallastown. The hail lasted for approximately ten minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure originating along the Gulf Coast moved northeast over the Appalachians, producing a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across central Pennsylvania. Heavy snow was observed over the Northwest Mountains with light to moderate snow and sleet accumulations common across the Central Mountains and Susquehanna Valley. Occasional freezing rain and drizzle resulted in a glaze of icing in most locations with significant ice accretion over the Lower Susquehanna Valley.||Widespread hazardous winter weather resulted in numerous vehicle accidents and road closures. Sporadic power outages were also reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow and sleet along with a light glaze of ice was reported across Northern Clinton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upslope southeast flow around an intense low pressure centered in Iowa on Christmas morning (the 25th) produced some spotty freezing drizzle across the Poconos. Untreated roadways and walkways were slippery.

A low-topped squall line raced northeast across central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours before sunrise, producing damaging winds with measured peak gusts between 60 to 85 mph. The intense winds resulted in two injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 70 mph toppled a large tree onto a home on North Mine Road just east of Lebanon. One person was seriously injured as the tree crashed through the roof of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of wintry precipitation affected central Pennsylvania from the evening of the 8th through the early morning on the 9th as a fast moving winter storm tracked across the Great Lakes. Precipitation transitioned from a mixture of snow and sleet to freezing rain, and then eventually to plain rain by the mid to late morning on the 9th as temperatures warmed above freezing. Significant ice and snow was observed over the Laurel Highlands. Despite the appreciable ice and snow, impacts were somewhat mitigated by the timing of the event which primarily occurred during the overnight period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving winter storm produced four to six inches of snow followed by a quarter inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy snow to the region from the early morning through the late evening hours. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from six to twelve inches across the Laurel Highlands and south-central mountains. The heaviest snow fell across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania where up to two feet of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from eight to twelve inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying low pressure system off the New England coast and the Canadian Maritimes produced strong west to northwest winds across the higher terrain of Eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon of the 27th into the early afternoon of the 28th. Peak wind gusts averaged between 40 and 50 mph and knocked down some weak trees and tree limbs. Most of the highest wind gusts occurred during the morning of the 28th and included 50 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 43 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the central Great Lakes on December 9th. Strong southeast winds developed ahead of this system during the early morning hours of the 9th.  Winds gusted to as much as 60 mph along and near the south shore of Lake Erie beginning around 430 am.  Then, a strong cold front stretching south from the low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of the 9th.  Showers developed ahead of the front and moved across Erie and Crawford Counties early in the afternoon.  Brief wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported with a few of the stronger showers.  Damaging southwest winds also occurred behind this cold front.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds developed during the early morning hours of December 9th.    The strong winds were enhanced by terrain that slopes downward toward Lake Erie.  This downward slope caused the southeast winds to accelerate greatly as the lake was approached.  Damaging wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported along and near the Lake Erie shoreline beginning around 430 am.  Winds continued to gust to around 60 mph through 8:00 am.  Peak gusts of 62 mph were reported at Erie International Airport at both 4:32 am and 7:12 am.  Widespread damage was reported near the lake.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down.  Scattered power outages were reported with thousands of people losing power.  Around 7 am, seven cars on a moving westbound train were derailed in North East Township.  No one was injured in the accident.  Wind gusts were generally no more than 40 mph from mid morning through early afternoon.  Then, a cluster of showers developed ahead of an approaching cold front and moved across Erie County during the early afternoon hours.  Winds briefly gusted to more than 55 mph as the showers moved through.  The cold front moved through during the middle part of the afternoon with southwest winds rapidly increasing behind the front.  Peak gusts of around 60 mph were reported late in the day.  Winds continued to gust to as much as 50 mph through 10 pm.  Some homes and buildings in the county sustained minor damage during this event.  Most of this was from lost siding or roofing.  Much of this damage occurred before daybreak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, sleet and mainly freezing rain occurred over the higher terrain of the Poconos from the late afternoon of the 25th into the morning of the 26th. Steady precipitation began during the late afternoon on the 25th as a wintry mix over the higher terrain, but changed to freezing rain quickly. Freezing rain then persisted through the night and changed over to plain rain by around 7 a.m. EST on the 26th. Ice accretions on the plateau averaged around one third of an inch. Any untreated walkways and roadways were hazardous.||The freezing rain was caused by a surge of warm air, first aloft, arriving from the south around an intense low pressure system that was centered over the Mississippi Valley. While the warm air arrived faster aloft, it lagged at the surface and was the cause for the freezing rain until temperatures even at the surface rose above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northwestern Pennsylvania during the evening hours of December 27th.  Cold northwest winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop late in the evening.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported from late on the 27th through late morning on the 29th.  The first of these periods occurred during the early morning hours of the 28th with a second period during the afternoon and evening hours of the 28th.  A brief period of heavy snow was also reported on the morning of the 29th.  Light snow or flurries occurred between the periods of heavier snow.  The snow ended completely by late afternoon on the 29th.  8 to 12 inches of snow was reported on much of Erie and Crawford Counties.  A few locations saw nearly 20 inches of snow during this evening.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening hours of December 27th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then continued through midday on December 29th.  Heavy snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 28th, during the afternoon and evening of the 28th and also on the morning of the 29th.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Light snow or flurries were reported between the periods of heavier snow.  Snowfall accumulations across Crawford County ranged from 8 to 14 inches during this event with the higher amounts across the northern half of the county.  A maximum of 16.0 inches was measured near Meadville with 15.5 inches at Crossingville.  A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong arctic air mass brought sub-freezing temperatures and extreme wind chills to much of central Pennsylvania. This was one of the coldest air masses to affect the region in years. Overnight low temperatures fell to almost thirty below zero in some locations, while highs struggled to climb out of the single digits above and below zero. The cold outbreak closed or delayed many schools over the two to three day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures and brisk winds produced wind chills of fifteen to twenty five degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper low pressure system produced moderate to heavy snowfall over the Laurel Highlands. The heaviest snow fell from southern Cambria into Somerset County, or along and west of Route 219.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten inches of snow was reported along and west of Route 219 in Somerset County. The heaviest snow (in excess of eight inches) fell over the highest elevations with lesser amounts ranging between three to five inches common over the eastern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure originating along the Gulf Coast moved northeast over the Appalachians, producing a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across central Pennsylvania. Heavy snow was observed over the Northwest Mountains with light to moderate snow and sleet accumulations common across the Central Mountains and Susquehanna Valley. Occasional freezing rain and drizzle resulted in a glaze of icing in most locations with significant ice accretion over the Lower Susquehanna Valley.||Widespread hazardous winter weather resulted in numerous vehicle accidents and road closures. Sporadic power outages were also reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to twelve inches of snow was reported across Warren County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northwestern Pennsylvania during the evening hours of December 27th.  Cold northwest winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop late in the evening.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported from late on the 27th through late morning on the 29th.  The first of these periods occurred during the early morning hours of the 28th with a second period during the afternoon and evening hours of the 28th.  A brief period of heavy snow was also reported on the morning of the 29th.  Light snow or flurries occurred between the periods of heavier snow.  The snow ended completely by late afternoon on the 29th.  8 to 12 inches of snow was reported on much of Erie and Crawford Counties.  A few locations saw nearly 20 inches of snow during this evening.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening hours of December 27th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then continued through midday on December 29th.  Heavy snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 28th, during the afternoon and evening of the 28th and also on the morning of the 29th.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Light snow or flurries were reported between the periods of heavier snow.  Snowfall accumulations across northern Erie County ranged from 8 to 12 inches during this event.  A maximum of 12.4 inches was measured in Millcreek Township with 12.0 inches at North East.  Officially, 9.7 inches was measured at Erie International Airport.  A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the central Great Lakes on December 9th. Strong southeast winds developed ahead of this system during the early morning hours of the 9th.  Winds gusted to as much as 60 mph along and near the south shore of Lake Erie beginning around 430 am.  Then, a strong cold front stretching south from the low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of the 9th.  Showers developed ahead of the front and moved across Erie and Crawford Counties early in the afternoon.  Brief wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported with a few of the stronger showers.  Damaging southwest winds also occurred behind this cold front.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds developed during the early morning hours of December 9th.    The strong winds were enhanced by terrain that slopes downward toward Lake Erie.  This downward slope caused the southeast winds to accelerate greatly as the lake was approached.  Damaging wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported along and near the Interstate 90 corridor beginning around 430 am.  Winds continued to gust to around 60 mph through 8:00 am.  A 75 mph wind gust was measured by a trained spotter about two miles southeast of the intersection of Interstate 90 and State Route 531 around 7:00 am.  Lesser wind gusts were reported in central and southern Erie County.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down across the northern third of the county.  Scattered power outages were reported with thousands of people losing power.  Wind gusts were generally no more than 40 mph from mid morning through early afternoon.  Then, a cluster of showers developed ahead of an approaching cold front and moved across Erie County during the early afternoon hours.  Winds briefly gusted to more than 55 mph as the showers moved through.  The cold front moved through during the middle part of the afternoon with southwest winds rapidly increasing behind the front.  Peak gusts of around 60 mph were reported late in the day.  Winds continued to gust to as much as 50 mph through 10 pm.  Some homes and buildings in the county sustained minor damage during this event.  Most of this was from lost siding or roofing.  Much of this damage occurred before daybreak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain, with some snow at the start, began falling during the late evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th across Berks County and Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs. The rain fell heavy at times between 5 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged around two inches. The concentration of the heavy rain and some headwater snow melt in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the streams and creeks in the southeastern part of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow melt and heavy rain caused flooding along sections of the Manatawny Creek in Berks County. Poor drainage flooding also occurred. The Manatawny Creek at Spangville was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1230 p.m. EDT through 328 p.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 6.17 feet at 215 p.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.43 inches in Kutztown, 1.36 inches in Mohnton, 1.34 inches in West Lawn, 1.19 inches in Fleetwood and 1.16 inches in Reading.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system dropped light snow across Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of 31st. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. Snow began between 5 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST and ended around 11 a.m. EST. The snow changed to freezing rain and sleet around the immediate Philadelphia area just before it ended. The snow's timing coupled with high traffic volume resulted in numerous accidents. Several roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania including southbound Pennsylvania State Route 61 in Ontelaunee Township after a tractor-trailer collided with a Sports Utility Vehicle. There were also several injuries from people slipping and falling on the ice.||Specific snowfall included 2.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.3 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 1.8 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 1.7 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 1.6 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 1.4 inches in Downingtown (Chester County), 1.0 inch in Royersford (Montgomery County) and 0.8 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport. Ice accretions were generally one tenth of an inch or less.||The low pressure system that caused the snow moved northeast from Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st and reached Wilmington, North Carolina at 1 p.m. EST and was southeast of Long Island at 7 p.m. EST that evening.

Strong northwest winds occurred on the 29th after a cold front moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours. Winds decreased during the second half of the afternoon after a high pressure system from the Midwest moved closer to the region. Peak wind gusts occurred during the morning, averaged 45 to 50 mph and knocked down some weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered outages. PECO Eneregy reported power outages to 11,000 of its customers mainly in Bucks and Montgomery Counties. In Bucks County, in Doylestown, a 250-year-old Black Walnut Tree crashed into a hair salon and broke a window and damaged its roof. In Bensalem Township, a downed tree damaged four vehicles as well as the roof and a window of one home. In Berks County, Pennsylvania State Route 10 was closed in Cumru Township because of a downed tree. ||Peak wind gusts included 53 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 49 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County), 45 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Willow Grove (Montgomery County) and 44 mph in Coatesville (Chester County).

The combination of melting snow and heavy rain produced field, poor drainage and stream and creek flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania throughout the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area.  Rain began falling during the evening of the 25th and fell at a nearly steady rate. The heaviest rain fell during the late afternoon and early evening of the 26th. The rain ended late in the evening on the 26th. Event precipitation totals across eastern Pennsylvania averaged between 1.0 and 2.5 inches with the highest amounts in and around Philadelphia. ||The steady rain was caused by a strong southerly flow of air from the Gulf of Mexico around an intense low pressure system that spent much of the 25th and 26th in Iowa before weakening and drifting to the northeast. The mild weather also rapidly melted the large snow pack that was present in southeastern Pennsylvania. The rain ended after this low's occluded front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth with the Schuylkill River was above its 5 foot flood stage from 333 p.m. EST on the 26th through 539 a.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 6.10 feet at 145 a.m. EST on the 27th. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 730 p.m. EST on the 26th  through 306 a.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 7.82 feet at 116 a.m. EST on the 27th. Event precipitation totals included 2.16 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 2.06 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

A winter storm brought heavy snow and freezing rain to the Poconos and an accumulating snow to the Lehigh Valley before it changed to sleet and then rain. Accumulations averaged around 6 inches in the Poconos, 2 to 6 inches in the Lehigh Valley and around an inch in the northern Philadelphia suburbs and Berks County. Ice accretions in the higher terrain of the Poconos averaged around one-third of an inch. ||Snow began during the late evening of the 8th as far south as the northern Philadelphia suburbs and Berks County. As warmer air moved north, the snow changed to rain by around 3 a.m. EST in the northern Philadelphia suburbs and Berks County and around 4 a.m. EST in  the Lehigh Valley. During the transition in the Lehigh Valley, some sleet occurred. The snow changed to rain in the valleys and freezing rain in the higher terrain of the Poconos around 6 a.m. EST. In the higher terrain of the Poconos, precipitation changed to all rain around 9 a.m. EST. The rain ended by Noon EST on the 9th.||Schools were closed in Monroe County. Most schools had two hour delayed openings in Carbon County and the Lehigh Valley. The heavy wet snow caused spotty power outages in Carbon County.||Specific snowfall included 7.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 6.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 5.7 inches in Glendon (Northampton County), 5.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 4.9 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Springtown (Bucks County), 2.9 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.5 inches in Tatamy (Northampton County), 0.6 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County), 0.5 inches in Hamburg (Berks County) and 0.4 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County). Ice accretions in the Poconos were less than one quarter of an inch.||The winter storm was caused by a strong surge of relatively warmer and more moist air above a surface warm front associated with an intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes. A weaker low pressure  system formed on the warm frontal boundary early in the morning of the 9th near the Virginia and North Carolina border. This slowed the advance of milder air into the higher terrain locations.

A major winter storm affected central and southeast Pennsylvania on the 19th and 20th. A lighter accumulating snow affected the Poconos. Snowfall averaged around 3 inches in the Poconos, 6 inches in the Lehigh Valley, 6 to 12 inches in Berks, Bucks and Upper Montgomery Counties and 12 to 23 inches in and around the rest of the local Philadelphia area.  The 23.2 inches of snow that fell at Philadelphia International Airport was the second heaviest single event snowfall on record and the heaviest ever for the month of December.||Snow began around 1 a.m. EST on the 19th around the Philadelphia area, but took about twelve hours for it to reach the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The snow fell at its heaviest between 10 a.m. EST and 10 p.m. EST with the most bands of heavy snow across extreme southeast Pennsylvania. The snow ended around 1 a.m. EST in Berks County, but did end until around 8 a.m. EST farther east along the Delaware River.  ||A 51-year-old man died of a stroke while he was shoveling the snow in Havertown (Delaware County). A snowmobiling accident claimed the life of a 28-year-old man and injured a 23-year-old woman in Quakertown (Bucks County).  About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power during the storm. About seventy percent of flights were cancelled on the 19th at Philadelphia International Airport. Those that were not, had delays up to six hours. Flight delays continued into the 21st. The Philadelphia Eagles' home game on the 20th was pushed back to 4 p.m. EST so the stadium could be cleared of the snow. The winter storm postponed many planned local and high school sporting events. It came at an inopportune time for retailers as the Saturday (the 19th) before Christmas is usually one of the busiest shopping days of the year. Many malls around the local Philadelphia area closed early. Many municipalities in the southeast part of the state declared states of emergency. Since this storm occurred on the weekend, the number of traffic accidents was down. But, U.S. Route 209 in Brodheadsville in Monroe County was closed because of an accident. AAA Mid Atlantic said that the volume of calls for a Saturday was 64 percent higher than normal; many were stranded motorists. Many school districts either closed schools or had two hour delayed openings on the 21st. Some churches cancelled services on the 20th. Trash pick-ups were also delayed.  ||Representative snowfall included 23.2 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 19.0 inches in Folcroft (Delaware County), 16.5 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 16.3 inches in Exton (Chester County), 16.0 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 15.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 13.3 inches in Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 12.5 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County), 10.5 inches in the Somerton section of Philadelphia, 9.6 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 8.3 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County), 7.0 inches in Boyertown (Berks County), 6.0 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 5.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 5.1 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County)||The nor'easter responsible for the winter storm formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 17th. It moved northeast along the northern Gulf of Mexico and was just south of Mobile, Alabama at sunrise on the 18th. From there it moved northeast and continued to intensify and reached Savannah, Georgia around 7 p.m. EST on the 18th, near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 19th. It continued to move northeast on the 19th and into the 20th and by sunrise on the 20th was located about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey.

An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain, with some snow at the start, began falling during the late evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th across Berks County and Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs. The rain fell heavy at times between 5 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged around two inches. The concentration of the heavy rain and some headwater snow melt in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the streams and creeks in the southeastern part of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melting snow and heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and several creeks to flood in Chester County. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 933 a.m. EST through 345 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 9.86 feet at 1215 p.m. EST. The West Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Honey Brook was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1035 a.m. EST through 134 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 7.31 feet.  The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 4 p.m. EST through 1108 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 9.76 feet at 815 p.m. EST. The French Creek at Phoenixville was above its 8 foot flood stage from 835 a,m. EST through 1218 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 8.41 feet at 945 a.m. EST. The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 705 a.m. EST on the 9th through 1202 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 7.83 feet. Event precipitation totals included 2.07 inches in Unionville, 2.03 inches in West Chester, 1.90 inches in Kenneth Square, 1.80 inches in Valley Forge, 1.75 inches in Downingtown and Nottingham, 1.67 inches in Glenmoore and 1.25 inches in Honey Brook.

Strong northwest winds occurred on the 29th after a cold front moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours. Winds decreased during the second half of the afternoon after a high pressure system from the Midwest moved closer to the region. Peak wind gusts occurred during the morning, averaged 45 to 50 mph and knocked down some weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered outages. PECO Eneregy reported power outages to 11,000 of its customers mainly in Bucks and Montgomery Counties. In Bucks County, in Doylestown, a 250-year-old Black Walnut Tree crashed into a hair salon and broke a window and damaged its roof. In Bensalem Township, a downed tree damaged four vehicles as well as the roof and a window of one home. In Berks County, Pennsylvania State Route 10 was closed in Cumru Township because of a downed tree. ||Peak wind gusts included 53 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 49 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County), 45 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Willow Grove (Montgomery County) and 44 mph in Coatesville (Chester County).

A weak low pressure system dropped light snow across Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of 31st. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. Snow began between 5 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST and ended around 11 a.m. EST. The snow changed to freezing rain and sleet around the immediate Philadelphia area just before it ended. The snow's timing coupled with high traffic volume resulted in numerous accidents. Several roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania including southbound Pennsylvania State Route 61 in Ontelaunee Township after a tractor-trailer collided with a Sports Utility Vehicle. There were also several injuries from people slipping and falling on the ice.||Specific snowfall included 2.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.3 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 1.8 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 1.7 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 1.6 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 1.4 inches in Downingtown (Chester County), 1.0 inch in Royersford (Montgomery County) and 0.8 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport. Ice accretions were generally one tenth of an inch or less.||The low pressure system that caused the snow moved northeast from Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st and reached Wilmington, North Carolina at 1 p.m. EST and was southeast of Long Island at 7 p.m. EST that evening.

A winter storm brought heavy snow and freezing rain to the Poconos and an accumulating snow to the Lehigh Valley before it changed to sleet and then rain. Accumulations averaged around 6 inches in the Poconos, 2 to 6 inches in the Lehigh Valley and around an inch in the northern Philadelphia suburbs and Berks County. Ice accretions in the higher terrain of the Poconos averaged around one-third of an inch. ||Snow began during the late evening of the 8th as far south as the northern Philadelphia suburbs and Berks County. As warmer air moved north, the snow changed to rain by around 3 a.m. EST in the northern Philadelphia suburbs and Berks County and around 4 a.m. EST in  the Lehigh Valley. During the transition in the Lehigh Valley, some sleet occurred. The snow changed to rain in the valleys and freezing rain in the higher terrain of the Poconos around 6 a.m. EST. In the higher terrain of the Poconos, precipitation changed to all rain around 9 a.m. EST. The rain ended by Noon EST on the 9th.||Schools were closed in Monroe County. Most schools had two hour delayed openings in Carbon County and the Lehigh Valley. The heavy wet snow caused spotty power outages in Carbon County.||Specific snowfall included 7.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 6.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 5.7 inches in Glendon (Northampton County), 5.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 4.9 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Springtown (Bucks County), 2.9 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.5 inches in Tatamy (Northampton County), 0.6 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County), 0.5 inches in Hamburg (Berks County) and 0.4 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County). Ice accretions in the Poconos were less than one quarter of an inch.||The winter storm was caused by a strong surge of relatively warmer and more moist air above a surface warm front associated with an intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes. A weaker low pressure  system formed on the warm frontal boundary early in the morning of the 9th near the Virginia and North Carolina border. This slowed the advance of milder air into the higher terrain locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving northeast toward the Ohio valley produced a mix of snow and freezing rain across the region. Ice accumulations of one quarter inch or more fell across Garrett county Maryland, and parts of western Pennsylvania along and north of Interstate 80.

A late season winter storm system moved northeast just off the east coast and brought periods of locally heavy snowfall to southeastern York and Lancaster counties. Storm totals were generally on the order of six to eight inches, mainly along and southeast of Route 30. Heavier snowfall occurred along and east of the I-95 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow was reported across the southeastern half of Lancaster County, mainly along and south of Route 30.

An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain, with some snow at the start, began falling during the late evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th across Berks County and Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs. The rain fell heavy at times between 5 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged around two inches. The concentration of the heavy rain and some headwater snow melt in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the streams and creeks in the southeastern part of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt and heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding along sections of the Perkiomen and Wisshaickon Creeks in Montgomery County. Pennsylvania State Routes 73 and 29 were flooded in Schwenksville near the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek. An 80-year-old woman was rescued from Middle Creek Road in Douglass Township after becoming trapped. ||The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington was above its 9 foot flood stage from 744 a.m. EST through 125 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 10.89 feet at 1045 a.m. EST. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 817 a.m. EST through 513 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 9.8 feet at Noon EST. The main stem of the Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 914 a.m. EST through 210 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 4.59 feet at 1215 p.m. EST. ||Event precipitation totals included 2.10 inches in Ardmore, 1.93 inches in Greaterford, 1.91 inches in King of Prussia, 1.80 inches in Gilbertsville, 1.79 inches in Lansdale, 1.72 inches in Collegeville, 1.64 inches in Blue Bell and North Wales, 1.57 inches in Pottstown, 1,56 inches in Palm and 1.48 inches in Norristown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected central and southeast Pennsylvania on the 19th and 20th. A lighter accumulating snow affected the Poconos. Snowfall averaged around 3 inches in the Poconos, 6 inches in the Lehigh Valley, 6 to 12 inches in Berks, Bucks and Upper Montgomery Counties and 12 to 23 inches in and around the rest of the local Philadelphia area.  The 23.2 inches of snow that fell at Philadelphia International Airport was the second heaviest single event snowfall on record and the heaviest ever for the month of December.||Snow began around 1 a.m. EST on the 19th around the Philadelphia area, but took about twelve hours for it to reach the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. An area of high pressure early on the 10th gave way to an advancing low pressure system which traveled up the Ohio Valley. This system moved across the region during the nighttime hours of the 10th and then tracked to the northeast during the morning of the 11th. Moisture in the form of snow and rain overspread the area from southwest to northeast during the day on the 10th. Some of the snow was moderate to occasionally heavy for a time across northeastern Pennsylvania. As milder air worked its way into the area, the snow and rain mixed with sleet and then changed over to some freezing rain and plain rain. The precipitation ended from west to east during the morning hours of the 11th.||While most roadways in Berks County remained relatively clear on the 10th, the weather conditions helped cause some accidents, including one that closed eastbound Interstate 78 in Greenwich Township for more than an hour in the afternoon. The driver of the truck involved was taken to a local hospital and was in satisfactory condition. Road conditions worsened during the night of the 10th as more frozen precipitation occurred. Numerous ambulance calls in Lehigh County were received during the snowstorm on the 10th, which were for people who were injured from falls. This was due to ice that was still in place, but then new snow fell on top of it, which resulted in walkways and other untreated surfaces being extra icy. Snow emergencies were called by numerous municipalities on the 11th, which implemented specific parking rules. Meanwhile, roadways in Northampton County turned rather slippery during the 10th due to snow, which resulted in numerous accidents. The majority of the accidents were minor, although some involved injuries. Minor accidents were reported on Interstate 78.||Some specific snowfall totals included; 6.0 inches in Delaware Water Gap (Monroe County), 5.5 inches in Bossardsville (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 4.4 inches in Walnutport (Northampton County), 4.3 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 3.4 inches in Glendon (Northampton County), 3.3 inches in Middle (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 2.8 inches in Catasauqua (Lehigh County), 2.7 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 2.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh County), 2.5 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 2.0 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County), 2.0 inches in East Allentown (Lehigh County), 1.9 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 1.1 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 1.0 inch in Reading (Berks County), 0.8 of an inch in Trappe (Montgomery County), 0.6 of an inch in Villanova (Delaware County), 0.5 of an inch in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 0.5 of an inch in Lansdale (Montgomery County), 0.4 of an inch in Somerton (Philadelphia County) and 0.1 of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County). Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. There was then a break for a few hours from about mid-morning to midday, then another round of snow moved in, some of which was moderate. As some milder air moved in above the surface, the snow mixed with sleet then changed to some freezing rain before ending during the early morning hours of the 11th. The combination of temperatures below freezing, fallen snow and a little icing on top, produced slippery conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent pair of storms moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast during the 7th and into the 8th. One potent storm moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the evening of the 7th, while a secondary potent storm developed along a front off the New Jersey coast. Both of these storms combined into one large and intense storm early on the 8th across the Canadian Maritimes. Behind these storms, much colder air flowed across the region, and this combined with a tightened pressure gradient, which produced strong offshore winds. This caused blow-out tides to occur during the early morning hours of the 9th.||The water level at the Philadelphia tidal gage dropped to 1.84 feet below mean lower low water at 6:00 AM EST on the 9th then bottomed out at 2.08 feet below mean lower low water at 6:30 AM EST on the 9th. Blow-out tides begin at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

A couple of upper-level disturbances moved near the area on the 19th. One clipped the New Jersey coast with a period of mainly light snow, however a secondary one, which was tied to a main disturbance in the eastern Great Lakes, produced an area of moderate to locally heavy snow across portions of eastern Pennsylvania. The enhanced snowfall rates along with temperatures below freezing, produced very slippery conditions. The snow tapered off from southwest to northeast during the early evening hours of the 19th.||Dozen of people were left stranded for several hours during the night of the 19th in the Hamburg Field House (Berks County) following a multivehicle accident on Interstate 78 between the Lenhartsville and Hamburg Exits, which forced State Police to close a 17-mile stretch of the highway for about six hours. No serious injuries were reported in the crash, which occurred just after 4:00 PM EST on the 19th when a tractor-trailer jackknifed on the icy roadway. It was reported that vehicles struck the tractor-trailer, concrete median and one another. Some crashed and flipped, then landed on top of other vehicles. A total of 23 vehicles were involved in the chain-reaction crash. The highway was reopened at 9:45 PM EST on the 19th. A five-car accident occurred moments earlier in the westbound lanes and may have led the unidentified trucker to brake, potentially setting off the chain-reaction crash.||Some snowfall totals included; 5.0 inches in Towamencin (Montgomery County), 4.5 inches in Phoenixville (Chester County), 4.3 inches in Eagleville (Montgomery County), 3.5 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 3.5 inches in Lansdale (Montgomery County), 3.2 inches in Downingtown (Chester County), 2.8 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 2.0 inches in Bernville (Berks County), 1.7 inches in Williamsport (Northampton County), 1.4 inches in Somerton (Philadelphia County), 1.4 inches 1.3 miles southeast of Rockledge (Philadelphia County), 1.2 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh County), 1.1 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 1.1 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County) and 0.8 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens, with even some single digits across northeastern Pennslyvania. The minimum temperatures dropped into the single digits, however some areas where the wind was able to drop off considerably, mainly in the northeastern part of the state, dropped to well below zero. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values to zero and below zero for awhile across eastern Pennsylvania, especially during the nighttime hours. However, the higher elevations in northeastern Pennsylvanina experienced the full force of the brutally cold air mass as Mount Pocono had a wind chill of 21 degrees below zero for a time during the early morning hours of the 16th and 27 degrees below zero for a time during the early morning hours of the 17th.||The Allentown Rescue Mission (Lehigh County) reported that they only provided shelter during the arctic blast of the 14th through the 17th to only men who have vouchers from police. The Bangor Area High School (Northampton County) closed on the 14th because of frozen pipes. Students were dismissed at 9:15 AM EST because there was no water. The Safe Harbor homeless shelter on Bushkill Street in Easton (Northampton County) filled its 27 beds as people living on the streets seeked a warm place to stay. Officials at the American Red Cross Lower Bucks County Chapter on the 14th reported that they were ready to deal with the impending arctic chill. An area of high pressure early on the 10th gave way to an advancing low pressure system which traveled up the Ohio Valley. This system moved across the region during the nighttime hours of the 10th and then tracked to the northeast during the morning of the 11th. Moisture in the form of snow and rain overspread the area from southwest to northeast during the day on the 10th. Some of the snow was moderate to occasionally heavy for a time across northeastern Pennsylvania. As milder air worked its way into the area, the snow and rain mixed with sleet and then changed over to some freezing rain and plain rain. The precipitation ended from west to east during the morning hours of the 11th.||While most roadways in Berks County remained relatively clear on the 10th, the weather conditions helped cause some accidents, including one that closed eastbound Interstate 78 in Greenwich Township for more than an hour in the afternoon. The driver of the truck involved was taken to a local hospital and was in satisfactory condition. Road conditions worsened during the night of the 10th as more frozen precipitation occurred. Numerous ambulance calls in Lehigh County were received during the snowstorm on the 10th, which were for people who were injured from falls. This was due to ice that was still in place, but then new snow fell on top of it, which resulted in walkways and other untreated surfaces being extra icy. Snow emergencies were called by numerous municipalities on the 11th, which implemented specific parking rules. Meanwhile, roadways in Northampton County turned rather slippery during the 10th due to snow, which resulted in numerous accidents. The majority of the accidents were minor, although some involved injuries. Minor accidents were reported on Interstate 78.||Some specific snowfall totals included; 6.0 inches in Delaware Water Gap (Monroe County), 5.5 inches in Bossardsville (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 4.4 inches in Walnutport (Northampton County), 4.3 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 3.4 inches in Glendon (Northampton County), 3.3 inches in Middle (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 2.8 inches in Catasauqua (Lehigh County), 2.7 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 2.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh County), 2.5 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 2.0 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County), 2.0 inches in East Allentown (Lehigh County), 1.9 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 1.1 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 1.0 inch in Reading (Berks County), 0.8 of an inch in Trappe (Montgomery County), 0.6 of an inch in Villanova (Delaware County), 0.5 of an inch in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 0.5 of an inch in Lansdale (Montgomery County), 0.4 of an inch in Somerton (Philadelphia County) and 0.1 of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County). Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. There was then a break for a few hours from about mid-morning to midday, then another round of snow moved in, some of which was moderate to occasionally heavy. As some milder air moved in above the surface, the snow mixed with sleet then changed to some freezing rain before ending during the early morning hours of the 11th. The combination of temperatures below freezing, fallen snow and a little icing on top, produced slippery conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while some milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across far southeastern Pennsylvania during the mid to late morning hours on the 28th then expanded northward for a time. The dense fog impacted the local bridges, as the speed limit on the Ben Franklin Bridge was lowered to 35 mph because of reduced visibilties.

The combination of a low pressure system that moved off the Eastern Seaboard and an upper air disturbance that moved across from the Great Lakes, produced snow across the region from the night of the 2nd into the night of the 3rd. Snowfall ranged from two to four inches across much of the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and upper parts of Chester, Montgomery and Bucks Counties to four to eight inches around the local Philadelphia area. The heaviest snow fell across eastern Chester, southwestern Montgomery, southern Philadelphia and most of Delaware Counties and occurred during the late evening of the 3rd.||Precipitation started as light snow across the Philadelphia northern suburbs on the evening of the 2nd. Precipitation started as light rain around the immediate Philadelphia area. Precipitation changed to snow or started as snow by dawn on the 3rd across much of eastern Pennsylvania. Steady snow did not begin in Chester and Berks Counties until around Noon EST on the 3rd. The snow increased in intensity during the afternoon of the 3rd, but air temperatures around freezing kept much snow from accumulating on roadways. This changed as the sun was setting during the evening rush. Across the immediate Philadelphia area, the snow was enhanced by an upper air disturbance that made the overall air mass more unstable and capable of snowing at greater rates. The snow was falling at the rate of an inch or two per hour during the late evening. The snow ended from north to south. It ended in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley during the evening of the 3rd and by 3 a.m. EST on the 4th across the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania. ||It was described as a stealth snowstorm as most of the heaviest snow occurred when it had the relatively least impact on the area. But, one area that was affected was the nearby Philadelphia region as well as the Philadelphia International Airport. Almost 200 passengers had to spend the night of the 3rd at the airport because of cancelled flights. About 40 flights were cancelled the morning of the 4th. Nearly 100 departing planes had to be de-iced. The runways were briefly closed overnight to be cleared. One of the longer runways was closed again around 10 a.m. EST on the 4th for about half an hour so snow could be removed from the north-south runway. All Philadelphia schools were closed on the 4th. Hundreds of other schools around the local Philadelphia area were also closed or had delayed openings. ||Specific snowfall included 8.4 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 8.3 inches in Wayne (Delaware County), 7.0 inches in Media (Delaware County) and King of Prussia (Montgomery County), 6.6 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 6.5 inches in Swartmore (Delaware County), 6.0 inches in Chesterbrook (Chester County) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 5.5 inches at Franklin Institue in Philadelphia and Hatboro (Montgomery County), 5.3 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 4.8 inches in Longswamp (Berks County), 4.5 inches in Furlong (Bucks County) and Emmaus (Lehigh County), 4.0 inches in Valley Forge (Chester County), North Wales (Montgomery County) and Bethlehem (Northampton County), 3.2 inches at the Delaware Water Gap (Monroe County), 3.1 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 3.0 inches in Levittown (Bucks County) and Hatfield (Montgomery County), 2.7 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 2.6 inches in Morgantown (Berks County) and Glenmoore and West Chester (Chester County), 2.5 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.3 inches in Palm (Montgomery County), 1.8 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County) and 1.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County).||The low pressure system that contributed to the snow formed on a stalled cold front over the southeastern United States on the 2nd. By 7 a.m. EST on the 3rd, it was located about 200 miles east of the Delmarva Peninsula and at 7 p.m. EST that evening it was approaching Nova Scotia. An inverted trough associated with that low pressure system combined with an upper air disturbance to cause enhanced snow across the region, especially the local Philadelphia area during the evening of the 3rd.

An area of low pressure tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley during the evening hours of the 27th. This area of low pressure continued northeast and moved through the Ohio River Valley, and then into western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon of the 28th. The low pressure intensified as it entered western New York State by that evening. Snow began across the region during the evening hours of the 27th, and continued through the evening of the 28th. Warmer air aloft was able to effect some areas by changing precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain for a short period. Many locations across northwest Pennsylvania hio experienced at snowfall amounts between 8.0 and 14.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the evening hours of the 27th and continued through the evening of the 28th. The snow was heaviest during the daytime hours of the 28th with visibilities dropping at or below one half miles at times. Snowfall amounts ranged between 9.0 and 14.0 inches. Across far southern portions of the county, there were some brief periods of mixing of sleet and freezing rain. However, the mixed precipitation did not limit snowfall accumulations very much. Trained spotters measured 12.6 inches in Franklin Center, and 9.5 inches in both Pont and Edinboro. West to northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with occasional gusts in excess of 30 mph accompanied the precipitation. Widespread blowing and drifting was reported with drifts in some areas a couple feet deep. Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

An area of high pressure moved across the region during the 6th. This allowed colder and much drier air to settle southward into the Middle Atlantic region.
