In Philadelphia, freezing rain occurred late in the afternoon on the 6th and continued for awhile in the evening before changing to plain rain. This resulted in a light coating of ice. The main low pressure system then tracked to the north on the 7th, as a secondary low pressure center formed just south of Long Island. The two systems produced plenty of rain across the area, although freezing rain held on across mainly northeastern Pennsylvania through most of the 7th. The higher terrain struggled to get to freezing on the 7th. An associated cold front finally moved through the region during the evening of the 7th, which brought drier air into the area.||In Tilden Township (Berks County), icy conditions proved to be too much for a municipal truck as it crashed and went over an embankment while spreading salt on Hill Road during the 6th. The driver was not injured. Several minor accidents were reported around Berks County on the 6th as a result of the ice. Most schools delayed the start of classes by two hours because of the storm. Six school districts in the northern portions of Berks County cancelled classes for the 7th. Accumulated ice brought down utility wires onto Route 568 in Robeson Township around 6 AM EST on the 7th. The road was closed between White Bear Road and Ridgeway Road for about an hour. Only scattered power outages were reported by PPL Electric and Met-Ed. Icy roads during the evening of the 6th contributed to an accident where a pickup truck lost control on Route 737 in Greenwich Township and struck a pole. No injuries resulted from the crash.||The storm system brought around a half an inch of ice on everything during the night of the 6th and the morning of the 7th. This contributed to numerous school closings and slick driving conditions in many municipalities of Carbon and Monroe Counties. It was reported that road conditions deteriorated quickly after freezing rain moved in around 4:30 PM EST on the 6th. It was reported that Weatherly Borough issued a snow ban the morning of the 6th. All schools in Carbon, Northampton, Lehigh and Monroe counties were closed on the 7th due to the weather conditions. The icing contributed to accidents, a fallen tree and minimal power outages throughout Carbon County. A tree reportedly fell in Andreas, in the area of the post office and the Carbon County line. Most roads in Carbon County during the night of the 6th and the morning of the 7th were slick in spots but passable. Many parking lots and sidewalks however were covered with a sheet of ice. The icy roadways caused numerous accidents during the morning of the 7th. An ice accumulation brought down a tree branch onto a power line on the 6th and 7th behind the 200 block of West White Street in Summit Hill. The power line blocked an alley way for several days until the line was repaired. In the Saylorsburg area (Monroe County), large tree limbs were reported down due to ice accumulations of up to one half of an inch. This also resulted in power outages.||Some school districts in Lehigh County had an early dismissal early on the 6th in anticipation of the winter storm. Students across Lehigh County began their day on the 7th with a two-hour delay. However, after a slow melting of the ice, especially on back roads and sidewalks, the districts decided to be cautious and cancelled school for the day. PPL Electric reported a total of 22 customers in Mahoning Township and two customers in Penn Forest Township were without power on the morning of the 7th. Freezing rain produced icy road conditions in Northampton County on the 6th and was blamed for a car crash in Northampton, along Seemsville Road at about 7:40 PM EST. The vehicle slid off to the west side of the road and struck a utility pole. The driver was uninjured. Nearly every school in the area was closed on the 7th due to a concern about icy back roads and sidewalks. In Bushkill Township, it was reported to be socked in with ice.||Some specific ice amounts include; 0.50 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 0.50 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 0.40 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County), 0.40 inches in Berlinsville and Easton (Northampton County), 0.40 inches in Saylorsburg and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 0.40 inches in Christmansville (Carbon County), 0.30 inches in Chester Springs (Chester COunty), 0.25 inches in West Rockhill (Bucks County), 0.20 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County, also 0.5 inches of sleet), 0.20 inches in Coatesville (Chester County), 0.20 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 0.20 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh County), and 0.15 inches in Willow Grove (Montgomery County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain overspread the area during the early evening hours of the 6th and mixed with some sleet initially. Ice accumulated up to 0.40 inches, which caused pockets of power outages and also produced areas of slippery conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved across the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th as strong low pressure moved north across the Great Lakes. A convective line along the cold front produced widespread damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland with thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH and a peak wind gust of 92 MPH at Allegheny County Airport near Pittsburgh. High winds gusting to 60 MPH behind the front in a strong pressure gradient produced wind damage across the entire region into midday on the 12th. In all across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland over one half million homes and businesses were without power at some point during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees and power lines down in Grove City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging non-thunderstorm wind gusts between fifty and sixty-five miles per hour were recorded across central Pennsylvania following the passage of a strong cold front. The high winds produced significant damage across the region, toppling numerous trees and wires. Several homes and other building structures also sustained moderate to major damage. ||The Public Utility Commission reported that utility crews restored power to over four-hundred thousand customers. Allegheny Power, who serves customers in Pennsylvania, West Virgina and Maryland said that this power outage event was the largest in the company's history. Overall, the estimated property damage sustained throughout central Pennsylvania was nearly one million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph toppled numerous trees and power lines across York County. Downed trees and utility lines caused road closures in York and Lower Windsor Townships. Shingles were ripped loose from a few area homes. The high winds also tore a roof off of a building in West York and knocked out power to about thirteen-thousand Met-Ed customers. Three schools announced early dismissals due to the the power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with peak winds of 85 to 95 mph touched down in northern Lancaster County between Lititz and Ephrata. The tornado produced widespread damage and caused three minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 Tornado with peak winds between 85 and 95 mph touched down near Lititz, and lifted a few minutes later between Lititz and Ephrata. While the tornado path length was 1.25 miles, widespread downburst wind damage associated with the parent thunderstorm continued for an additional eight to nine miles toward the town of Denver. Overall, over 200 structures sustained damage. Significant tornado damage affected eight trailer homes, six of which were totally destroyed. Approximately thirty barns also sustained either moderate or major damage. Three minor injuries were reported as a result of the tornado damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system that moved off the Delmarva Peninsula produced light snow and sleet across eastern Pennsylvania. Accumulations were less than an inch. The highest accumulations were around Philadelphia. Snow began falling just after Midnight EST on the 1st and mixed with sleet at times in the southeast part of the state.  The snow ended by 9 a.m. EST that morning. Places farther to the north, the snow began later and ended sooner. Because this snow occurred on a Sunday morning, very few accidents were reported. The one reported injury occurred in Lower Macungie Township in Lehigh County where only a dusting of snow occurred. The driver of a Sports Utility Vehicle was injured after he lost control of the vehicle on Lower Macungie Road and slid into an embankment.||Specific accumulations included 0.7 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, Clifton Heights (Delaware County) and West Chester (Chester County). All other accumulations were half an inch or less.  ||The light snow and sleet was caused by a rapidly moving low pressure system that moved northeast from Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of February 28th to east of the Delmarva Peninsula at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of March 1st. A more significant low pressure system was about to follow for later in the day.

An outbuilding was destroyed with numerous trees toppled or snapped. Maximum winds were estimated at 80 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense snow band produced six to twelve inches of snow in about a six hour period around the city of Lancaster. The heavy snow started in the evening on the 3rd and continued through midnight, before tapering off in the early morning hours on the 4th. The heaviest snow (ten to twelve inches) fell over Manheim Township, where snowfall rates were likely sustained at nearly three inches-per-hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: TV meteorologists from WGAL, spotters and Millersville University reports indicated heavy snow in excess of eight inches fell along and east of Route 501 from Lititz to Hunsecker. The snow band arched to the east southeast along Route 30 with heavy snow reported from Eden to Paradise. Remarkably, a few locations picked up as much as a foot of snow in only a short duration.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving line of severe thunderstorms associated with a sharp cold front raced east across the Laurel Highlands and south central mountains during the late evening hours. The storms produced damaging winds between 50 to 60 mph, toppling several trees and wires from Bedford to Clearfield. Some minor structural damage was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds of 50 to 60 mph toppled numerous trees near New Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging non-thunderstorm wind gusts between fifty and sixty-five miles per hour were recorded across central Pennsylvania following the passage of a strong cold front. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved across the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th as strong low pressure moved north across the Great Lakes. A convective line along the cold front produced widespread damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland with thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH and a peak wind gust of 92 MPH at Allegheny County Airport near Pittsburgh. High winds gusting to 60 MPH behind the front in a strong pressure gradient produced wind damage across the entire region into midday on the 12th. In all across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland over one half million homes and businesses were without power at some point during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a large tree down in Plum which ruptured a 10 inch natural gas line. 200 residents were evacuated as a precaution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while some milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across far southeastern Pennsylvania during the mid to late morning hours on the 28th then expanded northward for a time. The local visibilities lowered to one-quarter to one-half of a mile during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||The areas of dense fog in combination with wet to slushy roads created tricky travel as the visibility was greatly reduced for awhile on the 28th. The dense fog impacted the local bridges, as the speed limit on the Ben Franklin Bridge was lowered to 35 mph because of reduced visibilties.

An area of high pressure moved across the region during the 6th. This allowed colder and much drier air to settle southward into the Middle Atlantic region. Meanwhile, an area of low pressure organized across the Tennessee Valley during the morning of the 6th and tracked northward to the northern Ohio Valley during the evening of the 6th. An abundance of moisture surged well ahead of this storm. Some areas experienced some sleet and snow to start as the precipitation intensity was rather light. As the precipitation intensified and increased in areal coverage, freezing rain, sleet, rain and even some snow occurred. However, freezing rain and rain were the dominant precipitation type. Significant icing occured across northeastern Pennsylvania, with even icing reported in the southeastern part of the state. In Philadelphia, freezing rain occurred late in the afternoon on the 6th and continued for awhile in the evening before changing to plain rain. This resulted in a light coating of ice. The main low pressure system then tracked to the north on the 7th, as a secondary low pressure center formed just south of Long Island. The two systems produced plenty of rain across the area, although freezing rain held on across mainly northeastern Pennsylvania through most of the 7th. The higher terrain struggled to get to freezing on the 7th. An associated cold front finally moved through the region during the evening of the 7th, which brought drier air into the area.||In Tilden Township (Berks County), icy conditions proved to be too much for a municipal truck as it crashed and went over an embankment while spreading salt on Hill Road during the 6th. The driver was not injured. Several minor accidents were reported around Berks County on the 6th as a result of the ice. Most schools delayed the start of classes by two hours because of the storm. Six school districts in the northern portions of Berks County cancelled classes for the 7th. Accumulated ice brought down utility wires onto Route 568 in Robeson Township around 6 AM EST on the 7th. The road was closed between White Bear Road and Ridgeway Road for about an hour. Only scattered power outages were reported by PPL Electric and Met-Ed. Icy roads during the evening of the 6th contributed to an accident where a pickup truck lost control on Route 737 in Greenwich Township and struck a pole. No injuries resulted from the crash.||The storm system brought around a half an inch of ice on everything during the night of the 6th and the morning of the 7th. This contributed to numerous school closings and slick driving conditions in many municipalities of Carbon and Monroe Counties. It was reported that road conditions deteriorated quickly after freezing rain moved in around 4:30 PM EST on the 6th. It was reported that Weatherly Borough issued a snow ban the morning of the 6th. All schools in Carbon, Northampton, Lehigh and Monroe counties were closed on the 7th due to the weather conditions. The icing contributed to accidents, a fallen tree and minimal power outages throughout Carbon County. A tree reportedly fell in Andreas, in the area of the post office and the Carbon County line. Most roads in Carbon County during the night of the 6th and the morning of the 7th were slick in spots but passable. Many parking lots and sidewalks however were covered with a sheet of ice. The icy roadways caused numerous accidents during the morning of the 7th. An ice accumulation brought down a tree branch onto a power line on the 6th and 7th behind the 200 block of West White Street in Summit Hill. The power line blocked an alley way for several days until the line was repaired. In the Saylorsburg area (Monroe County), large tree limbs were reported down due to ice accumulations of up to one half of an inch. This also resulted in power outages.||Some school districts in Lehigh County had an early dismissal early on the 6th in anticipation of the winter storm. Students across Lehigh County began their day on the 7th with a two-hour delay. However, after a slow melting of the ice, especially on back roads and sidewalks, the districts decided to be cautious and cancelled school for the day. PPL Electric reported a total of 22 customers in Mahoning Township and two customers in Penn Forest Township were without power on the morning of the 7th. Freezing rain produced icy road conditions in Northampton County on the 6th and was blamed for a car crash in Northampton, along Seemsville Road at about 7:40 PM EST. The vehicle slid off to the west side of the road and struck a utility pole. The driver was uninjured. Nearly every school in the area was closed on the 7th due to a concern about icy back roads and sidewalks. In Bushkill Township, it was reported to be socked in with ice.||Some specific ice amounts include; 0.50 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 0.50 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 0.40 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County), 0.40 inches in Berlinsville and Easton (Northampton County), 0.40 inches in Saylorsburg and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 0.40 inches in Christmansville (Carbon County), 0.30 inches in Chester Springs (Chester COunty), 0.25 inches in West Rockhill (Bucks County), 0.20 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County, also 0.5 inches of sleet), 0.20 inches in Coatesville (Chester County), 0.20 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 0.20 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh County), and 0.15 inches in Willow Grove (Montgomery County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain, mixed with snow and sleet at times, accumulated up to one half of an inch. The one half of an inch ice accumulation report came from a spotter in East Nantmeal Township. Other ice amounts included 0.40 inches in Glenmoore, 0.30 inches in Chester Springs, 0.20 inches in Coatesville and 0.15 inches in Exton. Numerous trees were reported down in the northwestern portion of Chester County, due to ice accumulations of up to one half of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was draped from the southern Mid Atlantic region southwestward to the northern Gulf of Mexico. An area of low pressure developed along this front across the lower Mississippi Valley late in the day on the 27th. The storm system then moved northeastward and tracked near the Ohio River Valley during the morning of the 28th. As the storm continued to track northeastward, it strengthened as it moved into New England during the early evening hours of the 28th. An associated cold front then swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the early evening hours of the 28th.||A cold air mass ahead of the system allowed for snow to overspread the state generally from southwest to northeast during the 27th. However, there was a break for a time for many areas, then the snow began again during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 27th and fell moderate at times. As milder air then worked in, especially above the surface, the snow mixed with and changed to sleet and freezing rain during the night of the 27th. Enough warming occurred during the 28th, which allowed the wintry mix to change over to plain rain before ending from southeast to northwest. The icing held on the longest across the Pocono region as the low-level cold air remained entrenched across this area longer. The combination of frozen and freezing precipitation coupled with the cold air, created a high impact event and produced very slippery road conditions.||A tow truck driver lost control on an icy Route 73 in Earl Township (Berks County) at about 12:35 PM EST on the 28th. The truck careened town a hill on Route 73, then struck a guardrail, mailbox and another vehicle and both vehicles slammed into a house. The driver of the tow truck was seriously injured. The house suffered so much damage that it was condemned by the township. Fire police closed Route 73 for several hours. In another accident, the operator of a tractor with a plow was injured about 1:30 PM on the 28th when it collided with another vehicle in the 700 block of Mohrsville Road in Perry Township (Berks County). There was one serious injury. Several other vehicle accidents occurred around Berks County on the 28th with the bulk of them involved vehicles sliding off the roads into ditches or trees. Some accidents involved minor injuries although an accident in Wyomissing near the foot of the Buttonwood Street Bridge at 9:30 AM EST on the 28th featured a car that flipped over and the driver had to be rescued. The driver suffered a head injury. All Berks County school districts were closed on the 28th because of the winter storm. Across Lehigh County, the winter storm created havoc on the roads late on the 27th and 28th. Schools cancelled classes on the 28th and some businesses delayed the start of the work day.||The winter storm produced very hazardous conditions on the roadways and every Carbon County school district was closed. Numerous minor accidents were reported throughout the area, but no major accidents were reported. A jack-knifed tractor-trailer caused some delays on the Northeast Extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike shortly after 9:00 AM EST on the 28th. PPL reported about 185 outages. The two southbound lanes of the Northeast Extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike in Monroe County was closed for several hours during the morning hours of the 28th after a tractor-trailer jackknifed on the snow covered roadway and crashed into the center median barricade. The crash occurred around 8:30 AM EST and there were no injuries reported. As a side note, numerous accidents with minor injuries were reported on the 31st due to icy patches, which followed the winter storm. Several minor car crashes were reported in Northampton County on the 28th due to icy conditions.|Numerous Montgomery County schools were closed on the 28th because of the winter storm. Private and parochial schools were also closed. Numerous accidents were reported across Montgomery County due to the snow and icy roadways, and the Northeast Extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike experienced several vehicles accidents.  ||The accumulated snowfall followed by a change to some ice then rain on the 27th and 28th produced slippery conditions in Philadelphia County. The only significant traffic mishap occurred on the Vine Street Expressway around 4:30 AM EST on the 28th when a jackknifed tractor-trailer forced the closure of the westbound lanes. All the lanes were reopened by 7:30 AM EST. Some arrival flights into the Philadelphia International Airport were delayed some 90 minutes. SEPTA reported only minor problems due to the weather conditions. All Philadelphia public and parochial schools were closed, as were more than 800 other schools in the immediate region. Primos Elementary School and 15 other school districts in Delaware County closed on the 28th due to the mixed bag of precipitation. Schools were closed on the 28th and government offices opened late in Chester County. The snow topped by an icy glazed produced hazardous conditions getting out of driveways and across sidewalks. Damaging non-thunderstorm wind gusts between fifty and sixty-five miles per hour were recorded across central Pennsylvania following the passage of a strong cold front. The high winds produced significant damage across the region, toppling numerous trees and wires. Several homes and other building structures also sustained moderate to major damage. ||The Public Utility Commission reported that utility crews restored power to over four-hundred thousand customers. Allegheny Power, who serves customers in Pennsylvania, West Virgina and Maryland said that this power outage event was the largest in the company's history. Overall, the estimated property damage sustained throughout central Pennsylvania was nearly one million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts between 55 and 65 mph toppled numerous trees and power lines across Blair County. The high winds ripped a roof off of a school and caused severe damage to an adjacent church in Duncansville. Downed trees closed roads in the city of Altoona, where high wind damage was rather extensive based on insurance claims. Some residents were without power for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high pressure moved across the region during the 6th. This allowed colder and much drier air to settle southward into the Middle Atlantic region. Meanwhile, an area of low pressure organized across the Tennessee Valley during the morning of the 6th and tracked northward to the northern Ohio Valley during the evening of the 6th. An abundance of moisture surged well ahead of this storm. Some areas experienced some sleet and snow to start as the precipitation intensity was rather light. As the precipitation intensified and increased in areal coverage, freezing rain, sleet, rain and even some snow occurred. However, freezing rain and rain were the dominant precipitation type. Significant icing occured across northeastern Pennsylvania, with even icing reported in the southeastern part of the state. In Philadelphia, freezing rain occurred late in the afternoon on the 6th and continued for awhile in the evening before changing to plain rain. This resulted in a light coating of ice. The main low pressure system then tracked to the north on the 7th, as a secondary low pressure center formed just south of Long Island. The two systems produced plenty of rain across the area, although freezing rain held on across mainly northeastern Pennsylvania through most of the 7th. The higher terrain struggled to get to freezing on the 7th. An associated cold front finally moved through the region during the evening of the 7th, which brought drier air into the area.||In Tilden Township (Berks County), icy conditions proved to be too much for a municipal truck as it crashed and went over an embankment while spreading salt on Hill Road during the 6th. The driver was not injured. Several minor accidents were reported around Berks County on the 6th as a result of the ice. Most schools delayed the start of classes by two hours because of the storm. Six school districts in the northern portions of Berks County cancelled classes for the 7th. Accumulated ice brought down utility wires onto Route 568 in Robeson Township around 6 AM EST on the 7th. The road was closed between White Bear Road and Ridgeway Road for about an hour. Only scattered power outages were reported by PPL Electric and Met-Ed. Icy roads during the evening of the 6th contributed to an accident where a pickup truck lost control on Route 737 in Greenwich Township and struck a pole. No injuries resulted from the crash.||The storm system brought around a half an inch of ice on everything during the night of the 6th and the morning of the 7th. This contributed to numerous school closings and slick driving conditions in many municipalities of Carbon and Monroe Counties. It was reported that road conditions deteriorated quickly after freezing rain moved in around 4:30 PM EST on the 6th. It was reported that Weatherly Borough issued a snow ban the morning of the 6th. All schools in Carbon, Northampton, Lehigh and Monroe counties were closed on the 7th due to the weather conditions. The icing contributed to accidents, a fallen tree and minimal power outages throughout Carbon County. A tree reportedly fell in Andreas, in the area of the post office and the Carbon County line. Most roads in Carbon County during the night of the 6th and the morning of the 7th were slick in spots but passable. Many parking lots and sidewalks however were covered with a sheet of ice. The icy roadways caused numerous accidents during the morning of the 7th. An ice accumulation brought down a tree branch onto a power line on the 6th and 7th behind the 200 block of West White Street in Summit Hill. The power line blocked an alley way for several days until the line was repaired. In the Saylorsburg area (Monroe County), large tree limbs were reported down due to ice accumulations of up to one half of an inch. This also resulted in power outages.||Some school districts in Lehigh County had an early dismissal early on the 6th in anticipation of the winter storm. Students across Lehigh County began their day on the 7th with a two-hour delay. However, after a slow melting of the ice, especially on back roads and sidewalks, the districts decided to be cautious and cancelled school for the day. PPL Electric reported a total of 22 customers in Mahoning Township and two customers in Penn Forest Township were without power on the morning of the 7th. Freezing rain produced icy road conditions in Northampton County on the 6th and was blamed for a car crash in Northampton, along Seemsville Road at about 7:40 PM EST. The vehicle slid off to the west side of the road and struck a utility pole. The driver was uninjured. Nearly every school in the area was closed on the 7th due to a concern about icy back roads and sidewalks. In Bushkill Township, it was reported to be socked in with ice.||Some specific ice amounts include; 0.50 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 0.50 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 0.40 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County), 0.40 inches in Berlinsville and Easton (Northampton County), 0.40 inches in Saylorsburg and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 0.40 inches in Christmansville (Carbon County), 0.30 inches in Chester Springs (Chester COunty), 0.25 inches in West Rockhill (Bucks County), 0.20 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County, also 0.5 inches of sleet), 0.20 inches in Coatesville (Chester County), 0.20 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 0.20 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh County), and 0.15 inches in Willow Grove (Montgomery County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain, mixed with snow and sleet at times, accumulated up to 0.25 inches across Bucks County. The icing had an impact on roadways, with the higher amounts of icing had occurred in the upper portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while some milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across far southeastern Pennsylvania during the mid to late morning hours on the 28th then expanded northward for a time. The local visibilities lowered to one-quarter to one-half of a mile during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||The areas of dense fog in combination with wet to slushy roads created tricky travel as the visibility was greatly reduced for awhile on the 28th. The dense fog impacted the local bridges, as the speed limit on the Ben Franklin Bridge was lowered to 35 mph because of reduced visibilties.

A fast-moving line of severe thunderstorms associated with a sharp cold front raced east across the Laurel Highlands and south central mountains during the late evening hours. The storms produced damaging winds between 50 to 60 mph, toppling several trees and wires from Bedford to Clearfield. Some minor structural damage was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds of 50 to 60 mph toppled a large tree, which fell onto a house in Geistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which followed an unseasonably warm and humid late March day helped trigger severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 29th. Most of the severe weather was large hail, but there were about a half dozen reports of damaging winds in Berks, Delaware, Lehigh and Montgomery Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped nickel to quarter size hail in Vinemont (Spring Township), West Lawn and Shillington Boroughs and also within Reading. The Reading Regional Airport also reported a wind gust to 54 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved across the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th as strong low pressure moved north across the Great Lakes. A convective line along the cold front produced widespread damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland with thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH and a peak wind gust of 92 MPH at Allegheny County Airport near Pittsburgh. High winds gusting to 60 MPH behind the front in a strong pressure gradient produced wind damage across the entire region into midday on the 12th. In all across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland over one half million homes and businesses were without power at some point during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall caused flooding due to ice jams across parts of northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on Neshannock Creek caused flooding and evacuatins along Algoma lane. 5 to 11 houses were affected. 3 persons evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall caused flooding due to ice jams across parts of northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused flooding on Slippery Rock Creek near the SR 19 bridge. A cottage was flooded and water was in the yards of a few homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall caused flooding due to ice jams across parts of northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused Red Bank Creek to flood along Rt 28 between Summereville and Baxter. The ice jam broke in about 3 hours with water quickly receding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon of the 19th into the afternoon of the 20th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph.| |The persistent strong winds caused a tree to be knocked down in West Goshen (Chester County) on East Marshall Street on the afternoon of the 20th. Traffic was reduced to one lane in both directions.||Peak wind gusts included 46 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 45 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and West Grove (Chester County), 44 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 41 mph in Reading (Berks County). The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that went slowly northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system that approached from the Mississippi Valley.

Strong and gusty south to southwest winds occurred over the higher terrain in Chester County during the day on the 27th. A peak wind gust of 49 mph was measured in West Grove. The peak wind gust that day in Reading (Berks County) was 41 mph and at the Philadelphia International Airport 40 mph. The strong winds occurred in the southerly flow that preceded a cold front.||While there were a couple of wind events during the month of February, there was not much precipitation in Pennsylvania. It was the driest February on record in Reading (0.47 inches), the second driest February on record at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (0.78 inches), the third driest February on record at the Philadelphia International Airport (0.84 inches) and the fourth driest February on record in Mount Pocono (0.85 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds affected higher terrain locations of the Poconos from the afternoon of the 22nd into the afternoon of the 23rd. Peak wind gusts were around 45 mph and included 46 mph at Mount Pocono (Monroe County). The strong winds began after a cold frontal passage during the afternoon of the 22nd. Most of the peak wind gusts occurred during the evening of the 22nd into the early morning of the 23rd. Winds did not diminish until the evening of the 23rd. The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system as it moved from the Great Lakes and through the Canadian Maritimes and a strong high pressure system that was building east from the Upper Mississippi Valley.||While there were a couple of wind events during the month of February, there was not much precipitation in Eastern Pennsylvania. It was the driest February on record in Reading (0.47 inches), the second driest February on record at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (0.78 inches), the third driest February on record at the Philadelphia International Airport (0.84 inches) and the fourth driest February on record in Mount Pocono (0.85 inches).
Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees and multiple utility poles and power lines were downed by peak wind gusts estimated between 75 and 80 mph. Some trees fell onto houses in the Pleasant Acres Development. A roof was also blown off a storage shed. A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service determined that the storm damage was caused by straight line winds. The damage started near Route 24/Mount Zion Road and ended near the town of Hallam.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread downburst winds of 70 to 80 mph produced damage from a few miles east-northeast of Lititz to the town of Denver. Several hundred homes and structures sustained moderate to major damage.

The combination of a low pressure system that moved off the Eastern Seaboard and an upper air disturbance that moved across from the Great Lakes, produced snow across the region from the night of the 2nd into the night of the 3rd. Snowfall ranged from two to four inches across much of the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and upper parts of Chester, Montgomery and Bucks Counties to four to eight inches around the local Philadelphia area. The heaviest snow fell across eastern Chester, southwestern Montgomery, southern Philadelphia and most of Delaware Counties and occurred during the late evening of the 3rd.||Precipitation started as light snow across the Philadelphia northern suburbs on the evening of the 2nd. Precipitation started as light rain around the immediate Philadelphia area. Precipitation changed to snow or started as snow by dawn on the 3rd across much of eastern Pennsylvania. Steady snow did not begin in Chester and Berks Counties until around Noon EST on the 3rd. The snow increased in intensity during the afternoon of the 3rd, but air temperatures around freezing kept much snow from accumulating on roadways. This changed as the sun was setting during the evening rush. Across the immediate Philadelphia area, the snow was enhanced by an upper air disturbance that made the overall air mass more unstable and capable of snowing at greater rates. The snow was falling at the rate of an inch or two per hour during the late evening. The snow ended from north to south. It ended in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley during the evening of the 3rd and by 3 a.m. EST on the 4th across the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania. ||It was described as a stealth snowstorm as most of the heaviest snow occurred when it had the relatively least impact on the area. But, one area that was affected was the nearby Philadelphia region as well as the Philadelphia International Airport. Almost 200 passengers had to spend the night of the 3rd at the airport because of cancelled flights. About 40 flights were cancelled the morning of the 4th. Nearly 100 departing planes had to be de-iced. The runways were briefly closed overnight to be cleared. One of the longer runways was closed again around 10 a.m. EST on the 4th for about half an hour so snow could be removed from the north-south runway. All Philadelphia schools were closed on the 4th. Hundreds of other schools around the local Philadelphia area were also closed or had delayed openings. ||Specific snowfall included 8.4 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 8.3 inches in Wayne (Delaware County), 7.0 inches in Media (Delaware County) and King of Prussia (Montgomery County), 6.6 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 6.5 inches in Swartmore (Delaware County), 6.0 inches in Chesterbrook (Chester County) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 5.5 inches at Franklin Institue in Philadelphia and Hatboro (Montgomery County), 5.3 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 4.8 inches in Longswamp (Berks County), 4.5 inches in Furlong (Bucks County) and Emmaus (Lehigh County), 4.0 inches in Valley Forge (Chester County), North Wales (Montgomery County) and Bethlehem (Northampton County), 3.2 inches at the Delaware Water Gap (Monroe County), 3.1 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 3.0 inches in Levittown (Bucks County) and Hatfield (Montgomery County), 2.7 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 2.6 inches in Morgantown (Berks County) and Glenmoore and West Chester (Chester County), 2.5 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.3 inches in Palm (Montgomery County), 1.8 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County) and 1.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County).||The low pressure system that contributed to the snow formed on a stalled cold front over the southeastern United States on the 2nd. By 7 a.m. EST on the 3rd, it was located about 200 miles east of the Delmarva Peninsula and at 7 p.m. EST that evening it was approaching Nova Scotia. An inverted trough associated with that low pressure system combined with an upper air disturbance to cause enhanced snow across the region, especially the local Philadelphia area during the evening of the 3rd.

Strong to high winds affected Eastern Pennsylvania during the day on the 12th. The strong winds started shortly after a cold frontal passage between 4 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST and persisted through most of the day. Peak wind gusts averaged between 50 and 65 mph and knocked down numerous tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. Several roofs were torn away and billboards knocked down. Numerous vehicles were damaged by downed trees. Throughout the state of Pennsylvania about 250,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored in Eastern Pennsylvania until the 14th. In Eastern Pennsylvania, the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the northwest Philadelphia suburbs reported the most wind damage. ||In the Lehigh Valley, about 19,000 homes and businesses lost power including Westgate Mall in Bethlehem. In Lehigh County, the roof was blown off a garage in Allentown. A billboard was bent along U.S. Route 22 in Whitehall Township. Pennsylvania State Route 309 was closed because of downed trees. Many trees and limbs were knocked down along a five mile stretch of the Ironton Rail in Whitehall, North Whitehall and Coplay. In Northampton County, downed power lines caused a fire that totally destroyed a century old barn in Forks Township. Forty firefighters were able to contain the blaze from reaching other structures, although the heat caused the siding to melt at a nearby garage. A tree fell on and damaged a home in Bethlehem. An eighteen foot pillar was blown off the First Moravian Church in Easton. In Northampton Borough, the roof blew off a building at 14th Street and Newport Avenue. At least six roadways were closed in the county including Pennsylvania State Route 611 in Upper Mount Bethel. ||In the local Philadelphia area, PECO Energy reported about 85,000 homes and businesses lost power with Bucks and Delaware Counties suffering the most outages. All power was restored by the 13th. In Montgomery County, in Pottstown, the wind blew off the western facing portion of the roof off the four story building in the former Mrs. Smith Pies complex. The roof landed in a parking lot. A nearby family was evacuated until the roof was secured. The wind also started pealing away a roof of a home on Lincoln Avenue. Elsewhere a northbound tractor trailer was blown over on the Northeast Extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike just beyond the Blue Route Tolls. In Stowe (West Pottsgrove Township), the wind start to peel back the roof of the West End Fire Company. In Norristown, another home's roof was partially blown off and it took down a pole and wires. Signs were also blown down. In Lower Merion Township, a downed tree trapped a woman within her vehicle for over an hour. Another downed wire led to a raging garage fire which totally destroyed the structure. Numerous trees were reported knocked down in both Upper and Lower Gwynedd Townships. Within Philadelphia itself, the high winds ripped part of a portable classroom's roof away. It forced forty kindergarten students to evacuate; none were injured. A metal light post snapped at the corner of Broad and Callowhill Streets.  In Delaware County, in Lansdowne, a downed tree crushed part of one house and a neighbor house's roof. In Media, a couple of cars and fences were damaged by downed trees. In Chester County, downed trees fell on and damaged vehicles in Birmingham and East Coventry.   ||In Berks County, a tractor-trailer was blown off the westbound Pennsylvania Turnpike. The driver was not injured. In Mount Penn, about fifty tiles were blown off the five story Pagoda building. In Upper Bern Township (Shartlesville) the section of a tin roof in the rear of a hotel was peeled off. In Oley Township, roof damage occurred to homes under construction in the Meadow View Farms Development. Two homes were damaged by the winds in South Heidelberg Township.  In Reading, a billboard was knocked down along the West Shore Bypass. Pennsylvania State Route 724 was closed for one hour because of downed trees in Cumru Township. About 5,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was fully restored by the 13th. In the southern Poconos, about 5,500 homes and businesses lost power, but no home or business damage were reported.||Peak wind gusts included 64 mph in Forks Township (Northampton County) and within the city of Philadelphia, 56 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 55 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 54 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 53 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County), 52 mph in Reading (Berks County) and West Grove (Chester County) and 51 mph in Drexel Hill (Delaware County).|  |The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the state between 3 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

A wintry mix of snow and then freezing rain affected the higher terrain of Berks County and the Lehigh Valley as well as the Poconos from the late morning on the 18th until around Midnight EST that night. Snow began by Noon EST on the 18th. The snow changed over to rain in the valleys during the early evening of the 18th, but changed over to freezing rain over some of the higher terrain locations, especially in the Poconos. Precipitation ended during the early morning of the 19th. Snowfall averaged an inch or two, mainly over the higher terrain and ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch. The highest reported snowfall was 2.0 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County).||Untreated roadways were treacherous, especially during the evening commute. The wintry mix led to several accidents in Berks County both during and after the event was over. On the afternoon of the 18th, a vehicle skidded off of Hay Creek Road in Robeson Township and struck a tree. The roadway was closed until the accident was cleared. The driver was injured. During the evening commute, several minor accidents occurred. In Exeter Township, a two vehicle crash forced the closure of the westbound lanes of U.S. Route 422 for half an hour. ||After the precipitation ended and skies cleared. Fog and black ice formed on the roadways and affected the morning commute in Berks County. In Amity Township, the westbound lanes of U.S. Route 422 were closed for two hours after a two vehicle crash. A 16-year-old female driver was injured. In Tilden Township, a 17-year-old female was injured when the vehicle she was driving on Eastbound Old Route 22 crossed into oncoming traffic, went down an embankment and struck a tree. In Spring Township, a four vehicle accident occurred at the intersection of Pennsylvania State Route 724 and Spahn Road. In Reading, traffic was snarled in both directions of the Warren Street Bypass because of a pair of minor accidents.||The slick roadways caused some schools across the region to have delayed openings.||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure that moved northeast from the Ohio Valley through the Saint Lawrence Valley on the night of the 18th. Most of the snow occurred preceding this low pressure system's warm front while precipitation ended after its cold front moved through the region overnight.

Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon of the 19th into the afternoon of the 20th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph.| |The persistent strong winds caused a tree to be knocked down in West Goshen (Chester County) on East Marshall Street on the afternoon of the 20th. Traffic was reduced to one lane in both directions.||Peak wind gusts included 46 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 45 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and West Grove (Chester County), 44 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 41 mph in Reading (Berks County). The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that went slowly northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system that approached from the Mississippi Valley.

A mixture of light freezing rain and sleet fell across parts of Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and Poconos during the morning of the 26th. Precipitation started around 7 a.m. EST and temperatures rose above freezing after 9 a.m. EST.||The wintry mix contributed to many accidents, especially in Berks County. In Berks County, in Robeson Township, a Twin Valley School District Bus lost roadway traction on a downhill curve on Westley Road and struck trees on both sides of the road. Ten school children were injured, four needed hospitalization and one was seriously hurt. In Penn Township, a 35-year-old woman was killed after she lost control of her vehicle on Southbound Pennsylvania State Route 183, crossed the roadway and struck a tree. The road was closed for two hours. In Tulpehocken Township, a sports utility vehicle struck a pole and tree. The pole fell across the road which was closed until the pole was repaired. A minivan struck an embankment on Pennsylvania State Route 419 injuring a 32-year-old female passenger. In Greenwich Township, a 48-year-old woman was injured after her vehicle spun out of control on Pennsylvania State Route 737, went down an embankment and ended on its side. In Perry Township, the driver of another vehicle that went down an embankment off of Pennsylvania State Route 662 escaped injury. Several minor accidents were also reported on Pennsylvania State Route 737 in Oley Township.
Gusty northwest winds combined with unseasonably cold temperatures produced bitterly cold wind chills across the northern and western mountains of central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill readings of 15 to 25 degrees below zero were reported across Warren County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a dry March to date, breezy to windy conditions and low relative humidity levels made the 23rd and 24th vulnerable to fast spreading fires. Several brush fires occurred in Berks, Northampton and Monroe Counties. ||In Berks County, the largest fire started shortly after 4 p.m. EDT on the 23rd along West Benjamin Franklin Highway in Amity Township. Dozens of firefighters battled the brush fire, which took about three hours to extinguish. The brush fire burned about eight and a half acres near Hill Road. Traffic was disrupted during the evening rush hour. About 50 firefighters battled the blaze. Another brush fire started shortly before noon EDT and damaged a field on Treichler Road near Landis Store in District Township. Dozens of firefighters responded to the blaze, which burned brush and woods and threatened several buildings. Firefighters controlled it by 130 p.m. EDT. Brush fires also occurred on Carls Hill Road in Rockland Township at 4 p.m. EDT and Shed Road in Union Township about 540 p.m. EDT. Four other smaller brush fires were reported around the county on the 23rd. ||In Northampton County, brush fires occurred on the 23rd and 24th in Lower Saucon, Nazareth, Northampton, Hanover, Moore and Plainfield Townships. In Monroe County, a fast moving fire that consumed several sheds in Pocono Township spread quickly to several acres of brush. Firefighters were able to contain the blaze and save nearby homes.  ||The peak wind gusts on the 23rd were 36 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 35 mph at the Reading Regional Airport (Berks County) and Lehigh Valley International Airport. The peak wind gusts on the 24th were 23 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 22 mph at the Reading Regional Airport (Berks County) and 21 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

A cold front which followed an unseasonably warm and humid late March day helped trigger severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 29th. Most of the severe weather was large hail, but there were about a half dozen reports of damaging winds in Berks, Delaware, Lehigh and Montgomery Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm damaged a farm and several trees in Oley Township. The damaging winds tore off a portion of a slate roof of the farmhouse. It also ripped off siding and blew out several windows. Trees were snapped or uprooted in the area and two utility poles were snapped. About 130 of the 500 Metropolitan Edison homes and businesses that lost power in the county occurred in Oley Township. Other Metropolitan Edison outages were reported in Birdsboro, Fleetwood, Kutztown, Lenhartsville (the most), Mohrsville and Reading.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across Eastern Pennsylvania from the evening of the 1st into the evening of the 2nd. Snowfall averaged four to eight inches across the region, with some locally higher amounts around Philadelphia. Many snow emergencies were declared. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow made travel difficult and made it tough for road crews to keep thoroughfares opened. Numerous accidents occurred and major interstates were snarled. There were many fender benders, spin-outs and slides into other vehicles. The Mid-Atlantic AAA responded to 2,600 calls for assistance throughout their region. Most schools were closed on Monday March 2nd. Some universities and colleges in and around Philadelphia also cancelled classes. Many meetings and sporting events were postponed on the 2nd.||Snow began across the local Philadelphia area during the early evening of the 1st and spread north through the Poconos shortly after midnight EST on the 2nd. The heaviest snow associated with the nor'easter occurred farther to the east. Snow ended during the late afternoon, except during the evening in the Poconos. ||The worst reported accidents occurred along Interstate 78 in Berks County on the 2nd. On Westbound Interstate 78 about 8 a.m. EST, a one vehicle accident closed one lane for about 45 minutes. At about 9 a.m. EST, on eastbound Interstate 78, two vehicles collided and one person was injured. Within the hour another seven multi-vehicle accident near exit 35 (Lenhartsville) occurred. A 45-year-old man and a 42-year-old woman were injured. A vehicle that stopped before the crash was rear ended twice. A thirteen mile stretch of the eastbound interstate was closed from Shartlesville to Krumsville until 420 p.m. EST. The eastbound closures for a little while extended to Pennsylvania State Route 100 in Lehigh County. Farther to the west at 1030 a.m. EST, two tractor trailers collided on eastbound Interstate 78 and forced the closure from exits 19 (near Strausstown) to 23 (Shartlesville). Around Noon EST, two separate four vehicle collisions occurred on westbound Interstate 78. A tractor-trailer that stopped before one accident was struck by another vehicle and the driver was injured. The westbound side of the interstate was closed until 845 p.m. EST from exit 40 (Krumsville) to exit 23 (Shartlesville). Elsewhere in Berks County, many accidents were reported in Jefferson, Richmond and Wyomissing. In Reading, the West Shore Bypass (U.S. Route 422) was slowed to a crawl after a serious accident. | |In and around the local Philadelphia area, SEPTA reported day long 15 minute delays on their commuter rail lines. Amtrak had to cancel or cut back on a dozen Northeast Corridor trains. PennDot reported more than 50 vehicles became stuck on the interstates in and around the city. A tractor-trailer jack-knifed on the Schuylkill Expressway near Gladwyne (Montgomery County) forcing the closure of the westbound lanes for about an hour on the morning of the 2nd. About 245 flights were cancelled at Philadelphia International Airport. In Bucks County, a police officer was injured in a traffic accident in Bensalem. In Delaware County, traffic accidents caused about 3,000 homes and businesses to lose power. ||In the Lehigh Valley, a serious accident that resulted in an injury occurred in Wilson Borough (Northampton County).  In the Poconos, two tractor-trailers collided on Interstate 80 westbound in Carbon County. Two injuries occurred and the interstate was closed. In Monroe County, accidents were reported in Lehman, Jackson, Long Pond, East Stroudsburg and Hamilton. In Jackson Township, a woman injured her hand after her vehicle skidded off the road and into a tree.  ||Snowfall totals included 9.5 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 9.0 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 8.4 inches in Coatesville (Chester County), 8.3 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 8.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), Villanova (Delaware County) and Royersford (Montgomery County), 7.5 inches in Morgantown (Berks County), 7.0 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County) and the Roxborough section of Philadelphia, 6.7 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 6.3 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 6.0 inches in Exton (Chester County) and Boothwyn (Delaware County), 5.5 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Emmaus (Lehigh County), 5.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 4.2 inches in Reading (Berks County), 4.1 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 4.0 inches at Delaware Water Gap (Monroe County) and Tatamy and Forks Township(Northampton County).||The winter storm was caused by a nor'easter that moved east of the region. The nor'easter low pressure system moved from the Georgia and South Carolina border at 7 a.m. EST on the 1st to about 150 miles east of the Atlantic City coast at 7 a.m. EST on the 2nd. It was passing through the Canadian Maritimes during the morning of the 3rd. Heavy snow bands associated with the gravity wave occurred during the pre-dawn hours on the 2nd when most of the heaviest snow fell.

A strong area of low pressure tracked northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley through the central Great Lakes on February 11th.  A strong cold front trailing the low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania during the late evening hours of the 11th.  Strong southwest to west winds developed behind the front during the early morning hours of the 12th.  Hundreds of trees were downed and many homes and buildings damaged by the strong winds behind the front.  Most of the home damage was from lost shingles, but many homes also lost siding or gutters.  Scattered power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Erie County during the late evening hours of February 11th.  West to southwest winds gusted to more than 60 mph behind the front.  A 61 mph gust was measured at the Erie International Airport at 5:54 am.  Winds finally diminished after daybreak on the 12th.  Dozens of trees and a few utility poles were downed in the county causing scattered power outages.  Many buildings in the county sustained damage.  Most of this was from lost shingles, but a few buildings lost gutters or sections of siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front collided with an unseasonably warm and moist airmass, triggering scattered severe thunderstorms which produced damaging winds over portions of the Upper and Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at around 60 mph snapped off approximately 50 trees and uprooted 6 large trees along Skyline Drive in Jackson Township about 4 miles west of Millerton. The damaging winds also leveled a green house and collapsed a small barn. Some minor structural damage to a garage also took place on East Lawrence Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved across Lake Erie during the evening hours of February 18th.  A cold front trailing the low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania and ushered much colder temperatures into the region.  Lake effect snow showers developed during the early morning hours of February 19th and continued into the 20th.  The snow showers finally diminished during the daylight hours of the 20th.  The heaviest snow fell across inland portions of Erie County and the northern half of Crawford County.  Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were reported in those areas.  Westerly winds gusting to 30 mph at times accompanied the snow showers and caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed over Crawford County during the early morning hours of February 19th and continued through the afternoon of the 20th.  Most of the snow showers affected the northern half of the county where accumulations of 6 to 12 inches were reported.  A maximum of 14.8 inches was reported near Meadville with 11.2 inches at Canadohta Lake and 11.0 inches at Crossingville.   Most of this snowfall fell during the morning hours of the 19th and 20th.  Westerly winds gusting to as much as 30 mph accompanied the snow showers and caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Visibilities at times were less than a quarter mile.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which followed an unseasonably warm and humid late March day helped trigger severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 29th. Most of the severe weather was large hail, but there were about a half dozen reports of damaging winds in Berks, Delaware, Lehigh and Montgomery Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong to golf ball size hail fell in Upper Macungie and Lower Macungie Townships and Macungie Borough. The largest hail was reported in the Ancient Oaks area of Lower Macungie Township and it also covered the ground. Nickel size hail also covered the ground of Macungie Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which followed an unseasonably warm and humid late March day helped trigger severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 29th. Most of the severe weather was large hail, but there were about a half dozen reports of damaging winds in Berks, Delaware, Lehigh and Montgomery Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in Lansdale Borough and Montgomery Township in Montgomery County. No injuries were reported in spite of the damage. In Lansdale, wind damage occurred around West 8th Street. The roof was completely blown off the shipping building of the Village Handcrafted Cabinetry Company. The severe thunderstorm knocked down a chimney into the kitchen of a home near that building on West 7th Street and Maple Avenue. Water entered the home. Elsewhere in Lansdale, a utility pole was snapped at the West 5th Street Park and a large pine tree was knocked down on North Line Street. In Montgomery Township, about 25 homes suffered wind damage in the Weathersfield Development. Homes on Beacon Court, Essex Court and August Lane bore the brunt of the wind damage. The homes suffered siding and roof shingle damage. Whole trees were knocked down and damaged some vehicles. One home had its windows blown out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a dry March to date, breezy to windy conditions and low relative humidity levels made the 23rd and 24th vulnerable to fast spreading fires. Several brush fires occurred in Berks, Northampton and Monroe Counties. ||In Berks County, the largest fire started shortly after 4 p.m. EDT on the 23rd along West Benjamin Franklin Highway in Amity Township. Dozens of firefighters battled the brush fire, which took about three hours to extinguish. The brush fire burned about eight and a half acres near Hill Road. Traffic was disrupted during the evening rush hour. About 50 firefighters battled the blaze. Another brush fire started shortly before noon EDT and damaged a field on Treichler Road near Landis Store in District Township. Dozens of firefighters responded to the blaze, which burned brush and woods and threatened several buildings. Firefighters controlled it by 130 p.m. EDT. Brush fires also occurred on Carls Hill Road in Rockland Township at 4 p.m. EDT and Shed Road in Union Township about 540 p.m. EDT. Four other smaller brush fires were reported around the county on the 23rd. ||In Northampton County, brush fires occurred on the 23rd and 24th in Lower Saucon, Nazareth, Northampton, Hanover, Moore and Plainfield Townships. In Monroe County, a fast moving fire that consumed several sheds in Pocono Township spread quickly to several acres of brush. Firefighters were able to contain the blaze and save nearby homes.  ||The peak wind gusts on the 23rd were 36 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 35 mph at the Reading Regional Airport (Berks County) and Lehigh Valley International Airport. The peak wind gusts on the 24th were 23 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 22 mph at the Reading Regional Airport (Berks County) and 21 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed across the Poconos, the Lehigh Valley and Berks County during the early morning of the 15th. The combination of heavy rain that fell overnight and light winds helped form the dense fog. The fog thinned around 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th.

Gusty west winds prevailed across eastern Pennsylvania on the 10th. Peak wind gusts averaged 30 to 40 mph and caused isolated damage. In Berks County, in Cumru Township, a downed tree on Pennsylvania State Route 10 just north of Pennsylvania State Route 724 blocked the roadway from 4 p.m. EDT until 530 p.m. EDT. The peak wind gust on the 10th at Reading Regional Airport was 33 mph. The peak wind gust at Philadelphia International Airport was 35 mph. The strong west winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system moving through the Canadian Maritimes and a high pressure system moving east from the northern Plain States.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front advancing eastward through the Ohio Valley. The storms produced isolated wind damage and several areas of locally heavy rainfall from the late afternoon hours through the overnight period. Torrential downpours prompted numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in the city of Warren, eastward to near the Kinzua Dam. A rock slide was reported along Route 59 about 1 mile west of Kinzua Dam. Several flooded roads were also noted in and around the city of Warren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front advancing eastward through the Ohio Valley. The storms produced isolated wind damage and several areas of locally heavy rainfall from the late afternoon hours through the overnight period. Torrential downpours prompted numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding throughout much of northeast and east-central McKean County. Flooding was reported along Route 6, Route 46 and Route 155. Flooding was especially noted in the towns of Smethport, Port Allegheny and Marvindale, with roads closed and some basement flooding. In Port Allegany, the Medberry Manor Assisted Living Facility was evacuated as a result of flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front advancing eastward through the Ohio Valley. The storms produced isolated wind damage and several areas of locally heavy rainfall from the late afternoon hours through the overnight period. Torrential downpours prompted numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in central portions of Huntingdon County. In Smithfield and Juniata Townships, there was a report of a 200 yard wide area of geological subsidence due to a rockslide. Numerous roads were flooded and closed, with basement flooding and some road erosion. Route 22 was flooded. Two bridges were impacted with debris. The debris caused a diversion of a stream into a home which suffered moderate damages. Numerous road closures and basement flooding was also reported in Walker and Union townships. In Walker Township, Hartslog Valley Road, Station Road, Pond Road and Route 26 were closed due to flooding. In total, about 120 homes reported basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front advancing eastward through the Ohio Valley. The storms produced isolated wind damage and several areas of locally heavy rainfall from the late afternoon hours through the overnight period. Torrential downpours prompted numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused extensive flash flooding across central and western portions of Perry County. Numerous roads were flooded throughout the county, including Route 233, Route 850, Route 274, Route 34 and Route 74. Approximately 25 to 30 homes along Sherman Creek were evacuated due to rising flood waters. Five water rescues were performed. Over 100 basements were flooded. Much of the flooding occurred between Landisburg and Loysville. The flash flood event transitioned into a flood event, which lasted into the mid morning hours before waters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front advancing eastward through the Ohio Valley. The storms produced isolated wind damage and several areas of locally heavy rainfall from the late afternoon hours through the overnight period. Torrential downpours prompted numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the headwaters of Sherman Creek caused a sharp rise along the creek, and eventually lead to moderate flooding. A rise of nearly 12 feet in less than 6 hours was the result of between 5 and 7 inches of rain falling over a portion of the basin. Evacuations of 25 to 30 homes occurred along Sherman Creek due to the rising flood waters. Several homes were isolated due to flood waters, and some sustained flooding (basements, workshops). Flood waters rapidly subsided by late morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell along City Avenue in Bala Cynwyd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in Fordyce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon hours in association with a cold front moving eastward across the region. These storms produced a two reports of quarter-size hail and one report of thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail and damaging winds a few miles north of Strasburg. The thunderstorm winds snapped off a telephone pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving slowly across West Virginia produced widespread heavy rain and flooding across northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and western Pennsylvania. Rain amounts over a 24 to 36 hour period were from 2 to 3 inches on top of soils that were already saturated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported Tomcat Hollow Rd and Nilan Rd closed at the tunnel due to flooding.

Rain amounts over a 24 to 36 hour period were from 2 to 3 inches on top of soils that were already saturated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flooding on SR 481 in Monongahela near the Penos Plaza along Pigeon Creek. This flooding was a combination of back water flooding from the Monongahela River and run off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with torrential downpours formed along the warm frontal boundary in Berks County during the evening of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flooding in and around Richmond Township in Berks County. Roads were flooded and closed in the area. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 2.5 and 3.0 inches, most of which fell within one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually early heat wave affected southeastern Pennsylvania from April 25th through the 27th with high temperatures in the lower 90s each day. High temperatures also reached into the lower 90s on the 26th and 27th in the Lehigh Valley. The combination of the early hot weather and inadequate supplies of water led to more than two dozen people suffering heat exhaustion at a Gatorade Replay football game in Easton (Northampton County) on the 26th. Over 13,000 fans were at the stadium. At least two people were transported to Easton Hospital. The high temperature at Lehigh Valley International Airport on the 26th was 90 degrees.||The highest temperature during the stretch was 93 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and 91 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. This was the first April heat wave in Philadelphia since 2002. A cold front that moved through on the 28th (when temperatures fell just short of reaching 90) ended the hot weather.

A wildfire scorched a heavily forested strip of land nearly two miles long in Bowmanstown and Lower Towamensing Township in Carbon County. There was concern that the fire might damage some homes and buildings, but none were damaged. The fire was reported about 4 p.m. EDT and started behind the Bowmanstown Fire Company. Homes on Fireline Road were threatened as the fire traveled along the top of a ridge that runs the length of the street east toward Palmerton. Seven fire companies assisted battling the blaze and it was contained in about two hours.||The wildfire occurred on the last warm day in a stretch of unseasonably warm weather for late April. High temperatures in the Poconos reached into the 80s each day from April 25th through April 28th. Strong southwest to northwest winds occurred during the day on the 28th as a cold front moved through the Poconos. The peak wind gust at Mount Pocono (Monroe County) on the 28th was 38 mph from the westnorthwest.

A weak onshore flow from the Atlantic Ocean that preceded the passage of a warm front helped spread dense fog from New Jersey into the greater Philadelphia metropolitan area during the early morning of the 16th. The dense fog dissipated later that morning as a warm front moved north through the region.

Low pressure over Michigan moved slowly east toward the Canadian Maritimes. As the low approached late in the day, showers and a few thunderstorms moved into the area. Some of the storms produced isolated reports of large hail across the northern Poconos.

A deep upper level low over southern Ontario and associated cold temperatures aloft contributed to scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across central Pennsylvania. Several reports of large hail were observed during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees between Lincoln Falls and Hillsgrove just west of Forksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain and moist soil conditions caused flash flooding of smaller creeks and eventually flooding of a larger creek in Montgomery County. While some light rain fell during the evening of the 6th, the heaviest rain fell between Midnight EDT and 3 a.m. EDT on the 7th as thunderstorms moved through the region. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 2 and 3 inches across sections of central Chester, Montgomery and Bucks Counties. The thunderstorms formed along a warm front that was moving north across the region during the early morning on the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Valley Creek at Valley Forge exceeded its 7 foot flood stage from 256 a.m. EDT through 402 a.m. EDT on the 7th. It crested at 7.27 feet at 330 a.m. EDT. Chester County storm totals included 1.74 inches in Downingtown and 1.43 inches in West Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex, with origins from the Midwest and Ohio Valley, produced a quick one to two inches of rainfall over portions of the Laurel Highlands and south-central Mountains during the early morning.  This lead to rapid rises on small creeks and streams, which overflowed their banks and flooded several secondary roads. Later in the afternoon, isolated severe thunderstorms developed over the Lower Susquehanna Valley ahead of a cold front. These storms produced wind damage in York and Lancaster counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down large tree limbs in Dallastown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm snapped off about 30 trees near Cowans Gap State Park near the Fulton/Franklin County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours across south-central Pennsylvania, ahead of an upper level disturbance moving eastward from the Ohio Valley. At the surface, a nearly stationary frontal boundary extended southwest from central Pennsylvania back through the Ohio Valley toward a weak area low pressure centered over southern Illinois. This boundary had traversed the Commonwealth the day before as a cold front, and moved back northward overnight. A moist, unstable airmass was present south of this frontal boundary within a zone of moderate wind shear aloft. This environment supported organized clusters of storms which produced a few reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees a few miles southeast of York near mile marker 217 along Interstate 83.
