EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding across portions of the Lower Susquehanna during the late afternoon and evening of the 10th into the early morning hours on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported heavy rain and flash flooding in south-central York County. The majority of the significant flooding was in Codorus Township, North Codorus Township, Jefferson Borough, Seven Valleys Borough and Shrewsbury Township. North Codorus Township was most heavily impacted with 15 to 20 roads sustaining moderate to major damage and culvert and berm wash-outs. Some bridge surface wash-out was also reported. Approximately 8 homes had flooded basements, with one garage that also had some foundation damage. During the event, there were two water rescues where water had surrounded their homes. Their were no injuries. ||The onset of minor flooding which included street flooding began around 4:10 pm EDT in North Codorus Township in the vicinity of Shaffers Church Rd and Heindel Rd. The first flooded basement report was received at 4:40 pm EDT. ||Significant Flash Flooding began around 4:50 pm EDT, with water surrounding a house on Strickhouser Road. Roadways under water were reported around 5:37 pm EDT, including Seven Valleys Road and Cherry Street in Codorus township. At 5:54 pm EDT, water up to the first floor of a home was reported on Kraft Mill Rd in North Codorus Township. Significant flash flooding began in Seven Valleys Borough around 5:55 pm EDT where Seven Valleys Fire Station were evacuating personnel and equipment. Several houses in the area had basement flooding in the area.| |Spruce Road and Country Club Road in Shrewsbury Township were completely flooded at 6:04 pm EDT. The surface of a bridge along Route 616 in the area of Junction Road in North Codorus Township was washed away. Flooding peaked and water began subsiding after 9:30 pm EDT.||North Codorus, Codorus Township and the boroughs of Seven Valleys and Jefferson each declared disaster emergencies related to the flash flooding with overall damages estimated well-over 100K.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Management and local law enforcement reported flash flooding over portions of Franklin County. Three roads were closed in Guilford and Quincy Townships. One vehicle was stranded in Guilford Township. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours across south-central Pennsylvania, ahead of an upper level disturbance moving eastward from the Ohio Valley. At the surface, a nearly stationary frontal boundary extended southwest from central Pennsylvania back through the Ohio Valley toward a weak area low pressure centered over southern Illinois. This boundary had traversed the Commonwealth the day before as a cold front, and moved back northward overnight. A moist, unstable airmass was present south of this frontal boundary within a zone of moderate wind shear aloft. This environment supported organized clusters of storms which produced a few reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Richland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain and moist soil conditions caused flash flooding of smaller creeks and eventually flooding of a larger creek in Montgomery County. While some light rain fell during the evening of the 6th, the heaviest rain fell between Midnight EDT and 3 a.m. EDT on the 7th as thunderstorms moved through the region. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 2 and 3 inches across sections of central Chester, Montgomery and Bucks Counties. The thunderstorms formed along a warm front that was moving north across the region during the early morning on the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flooding across smaller creeks in central and upper Bucks County. The runoff and smaller stream flooding also led to flooding along the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek in  Montgomery County. Bucks County storm totals included 2.03 inches in Perkasie, 1.18 inches in Doylestown and 1.10 inches in Newtown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 7 a.m. EDT through 1007 a.m. EDT on the 7th. It crested at 7.27 feet at 830 a.m. EDT. Runoff from the heavy rain that fell earlier that morning in upper Bucks County and central Montgomery County caused the flooding. Storm totals included 2.03 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 1.91 inches in Hatfield (Montgomery County) and 1.26 inches in Green Lane (Montgomery County).

About one hundred cloud to ground lightning strikes occurred within a fifteen minute period in the Lehigh Valley. ||In Moore Township, a lightning strike and ensuing fire gutted a home on Countryview Lane. The shell of the house collapsed. Two adults that lived in the house were displaced and two cats died in the house. In Bushkill Township, a family was displaced after a lightning strike and ensuing fire badly damaged a home on Bushkill Center Road. The rear of the home around the kitchen was badly damaged. Two dogs were rescued. Other house fires were reported in Bath and Upper Nazareth Township. In East Allen Township, a family was trapped after a lightning strike downed wires around their home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex evolved southeastward from the Upper Ohio Valley and across southwestern Pennsylvania during the late evening and early overnight hours. Locally heavy rainfall of over 2 inches in a short duration produced rapid rises on area creeks and streams, causing them to overflow their banks. This resulted in flooded roads and basements across portions of southwestern Somerset County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Lower Turkeyfoot Township near the town of Ursina. Laurel Hill Creek rose over 4 feet and flooded Humbert and Jersey Hollow Roads. A number of basements were also flooded. Some additional minor flooding and flooded basements were noted south of Somerset.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy Rain caused Flash Flooding in Lower Turkeyfoot Township mainly near the town of Ursina. Laurel Hill Creek rose over 4 feet...flooding Humbert and Jersey Hollow Roads. A number of basements were also flooding in the area. Some additional minor flooding and flooded basements were noted south of Somerset. The Flash Flooding transitioned into a Flood event, as high waters remained for some time after the rain ended, continuing to affect roads and basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in State College confirmed a EF1 tornado about three miles north of Saint Marys in Elk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak EF1 tornado was confirmed in a mainly rural area just north of St. Marys. Much of the tornado was classified in the EF0 range. The tornado was on the ground for about 1.25 miles mainly over a wooded area. The tornado snapped off or toppled about 20 to 30 trees. A corn field was also damaged. A gutter and chimney were damaged on two homes near the end of the tornado track on Siecker Road. The maximum wind speed was estimated near 90 mph. No injuries were reported.

Rainfall amounts ranged from three to six inches in less than three hours with the highest amounts over eastern Cumberland, eastern Franklin and northern York Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Yellow Breeches Creek at Camp Hill to exceed flood stage. A number of homes in the Green Lane Farms development were affected by flood waters.

Several small streams and creeks exceeded bankfull including Muncy Creek along Routes 405 and 422 between Muncy and Hughesville. Nearly three feet of water closed roads in Muncy Creek Township near Clarkstown. Numerous low-lying and poor drainage flooding was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms along a nearly stationary cold front produced locally heavy rainfall in a short duration, resulting in flash flooding across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley. Rainfall amounts ranged from three to six inches in less than three hours with the highest amounts over eastern Cumberland, eastern Franklin and northern York Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced significant flash flooding across northern York County. Flooding closed numerous roads in Carrol Township. A water rescue was performed at the intersection of the Gettysburg Pike and Chestnut Street on the Rosegarden underpass, where two vehicles with 4-5 occupants were stranded. Junction Road bridge over the Dogwood Creek was jammed with debris, causing water to spill over the bridge and damage the road/bridge structure. West Siddonsburg Road between Route 15 and Orebank Road was closed due to flooding. A rock slide occurred on Mt. Airy and Ridge Road in Monaghan Township. Significant damage was reported on Guilbert Road between West Siddonsburg Road and Stony Run Road along the Cumberland County line. Route 74 was closed between Campground Road and Williams Grove Road. Fairview and Monaghan Townships each issued disaster declarations for the aftermath of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved through southeast Pennsylvania during the morning of June 9th. The squall line formed near a warm frontal boundary. In addition to the winds, the thunderstorms were prolific lightning producers. PECO Energy reported about 10,000 homes and businesses lost power that morning in southeastern Pennsylvania because of the combination of mainly lightning and some damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Fielding Drive man was hospitalized after he was shocked by a power surge caused by a lightning strike. Lightning struck the roof of a home on Rabbit Run Road in Willistown Township. It damaged some shingles on the roof as well as a nearby pine tree.

Fairview and Monaghan Townships each issued disaster declarations for the aftermath of the flooding.||The flash flooding persisted into the overnight hours on the 24th and transitioned into areal county flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper air disturbance triggered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. A few storms produced isolated wind damage occurred in Potter and Snyder Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down 30 pine and apple trees north of Austin on Upper Bark Shanty Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved through southeast Pennsylvania during the morning of June 9th. The squall line formed near a warm frontal boundary. In addition to the winds, the thunderstorms were prolific lightning producers. PECO Energy reported about 10,000 homes and businesses lost power that morning in southeastern Pennsylvania because of the combination of mainly lightning and some damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Honey Brook Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the severe weather, a squall line of thunderstorms that formed near a warm frontal boundary produced flash flooding in Chester County during the morning of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of Goose Creek in West Chester. Residents along the 300 block of East Union Street were evacuated until the flooding subsided that afternoon. No injuries or damage were reported. The morning event total in Downingtown was 1.90 inches.||The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain during the morning and afternoon swelled all creeks in Chester County. Two male kayakers drowned in the swollen Brandywine Creek on the afternoon of the 9th in East Bradford Township. One man was pulled from the creek at 210 p.m. EDT that afternoon. They were last seen on the creek near a four foot high dam north of a bridge near Creek Route and Pennsylvania State Route 52 in the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper air disturbance triggered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. A few storms produced isolated wind damage occurred in Potter and Snyder Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Penn Township just west of Selinsgrove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated severe thunderstorms produced wind damage across portions of south-central Pennsylvania during the mid to late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 70 mph tore a portion of a metal roof from a large barn and knocked down several trees in Dickeys Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated severe thunderstorms produced wind damage across portions of south-central Pennsylvania during the mid to late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 70 mph knocked down trees and wires near Mercersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated severe thunderstorms produced wind damage across portions of south-central Pennsylvania during the mid to late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 70 mph produced significant wind damage in Lemasters, or a few miles northeast of Mercersburg. The damaging winds tore shingles from a roof and propelled an empty burning barrel through a window. Several tree branches were snapped off, and fell onto the house. Siding was peeled back from the house and a residential yard fence was destroyed by a large tree branch. A few large trees were uprooted away from the house near a corn field. A small portion of the corn field sustained damage from both strong winds and falling tree branches. A pavilion at a local town community center off Steele Avenue was flattened when its roof collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall in a short duration associated with training thunderstorms produced flash flooding across portions of the Middle Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding just north of Watsontown in Delaware Township along Route 405. A trailer park was inundated by up to six feet of water with at least ten trailers flooded. One resident was temporarily evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving and intense low pressure system was responsible for strong northwest winds across Eastern Pennsylvania from the evening of the 3rd through the early evening of the 4th. Strong winds persisted through the night of the 4th in the Poconos. Most of the peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph.  | |In Pottstown (Montgomery County), the roof of a home at the intersection of King and Hanover Streets was damaged. The northwest corner of it was partially torn off. In Northampton County, about 1,500 homes and businesses lost power. Half of the outages occurred after a pole snapped in Bangor.||Peak wind gusts included 51 mph in Reading (Berks County), 50 mph in Walnutport (Northampton County), 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, West Grove (Chester County) and Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 46 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and Doylestown (Bucks County).||The strong northwest winds began after a cold front moved through the state during the evening on the 3rd. It persisted through the early evening of the 4th until the low pressure system associated with that front moved farther northeast up the Saint Lawrence River Valley and started to weaken.

A cold front moved into western Pennsylvania, with scattered showers and thunderstorms developing ahead of the boundary. Isolated reports of damaging winds occurred in northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A modular field office was destroyed at a truck stop at exit 217 off Interstate 81.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front triggered widely scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. A few storms produced isolated wind damage across Lycoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Perryville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in State College confirmed an EF0 tornado near White Deer Furnace in Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado, with maximum wind speeds estimated around 75 mph, touched down on the north side of Interstate 80 and moved southeast for approximately one mile. The tornado crossed Interstate 80 before lifting near White Deer Furnace. Damage was minimal with about ten trees and several large branches blown down or uprooted. Eye witnesses, including a Pennsylvania State Trooper, reported broad rotation within the storm and a funnel reaching the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front advancing eastward through the Ohio Valley. The storms produced isolated wind damage and several areas of locally heavy rainfall from the late afternoon hours through the overnight period. Torrential downpours prompted numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down trees near Bear Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated around 60 mph uprooted or broke off several trees and limbs in Portage Borough. Two homes, vehicles and fences all sustained damage. The also winds knocked down wires causing isolated power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cool air associated with a strong upper level low over the Great Lakes region combined with warm Lake Erie waters to produce persistent, intense lake-enhanced rainfall which trained repeatedly over northern Erie County, Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 30th.  Over 4 inches of rain fell along the Interstate 90 corridor from near Girard to just south of Erie, with isolated locations approaching 7 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers formed over Erie County between 4 and 5 am local daylight time, bringing two to locally three inches of rain through 7am across the Interstate 90 corridor in northern Erie County.  As the morning progressed, more and more roadways slowly became impassable, with closures reported at Higler Road, Avoinia Road (Fairview), Gunnison Road (Greene Twp), and at Interchange Road between I-79 and US-19.  Debris began to accumulate in many creekbeds due to the high water.  Rainfall rates slackened between 7am and 9am, allowing water levels to start a slow recession.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cool air associated with a strong upper level low over the Great Lakes region combined with warm Lake Erie waters to produce persistent, intense lake-enhanced rainfall which trained repeatedly over northern Erie County, Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 30th.  Over 4 inches of rain fell along the Interstate 90 corridor from near Girard to just south of Erie, with isolated locations approaching 7 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ongoing, persistent line of lake-enhanced showers re-intensified around 9am local daylight time and shifted north of Interstate 90, inducing rapid rises along Mill Creek in the City of Erie as well as in many neighborhoods on the south side of town.  Especially hard hit were areas in Belle Valley along Mill Creek, which quickly rose out of its banks, flooding roadways and over 50 homes near East Gore Road.  The Belle Valley Fire Station, located at Norcross Road and State Route 8, flooded to a depth of 6 feet within minutes around 9:30am as Mill Creek rose out of its banks.  800 feet of Henderson Road was washed out just upstream from Belle Valley, while downstream, high water from Mill Creek flooded the Erie Zoo and Glenwood Park Avenue.  Flooding was widespread in residential areas between Interstate 79 and Mill Creek, as storm sewers were overwhelmed by the additional heavy rainfall.  Emergency personnel responded to well over 50 calls for rescue assistance from stranded motorists and homeowners.  At 10:30am, City of Erie authorities requested that non-essential travel stop due to the widespread flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front retreating to the north and a cold front approaching from the west produced strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 29th. One EF-2 tornado also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same parent severe thunderstorm that evolved into a tornado farther east in Monroe County caused wind damage at the Pocono Wildlife Rehab Center in Hamilton Township. The damaging winds ripped the roof off the birds of prey pen. A downed tree also damaged a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rains that moved across the same parts of the Poconos produced flooding during the afternoon and evening of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain forced some small streams out of their banks in Pocono Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were around three inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cool air associated with a strong upper level low over the Great Lakes region combined with warm Lake Erie waters to produce persistent, intense lake-enhanced rainfall which trained repeatedly over northern Erie County, Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 30th.  Over 4 inches of rain fell along the Interstate 90 corridor from near Girard to just south of Erie, with isolated locations approaching 7 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ongoing, persistent line of lake-enhanced showers re-intensified around 9am local daylight time and shifted north of Interstate 90, inducing rapid rises along Fourmile Creek in Wesleyville, Harborcreek Township, and Lawrence Park.  Residences along Mindi Court in the Spring Creek Mobile Home Park were flooded as Fourmile Creek rose 4 to 5 feet out of bank.  Fourmile Creek also flooded East Lake Road (State Route 5) in Lawrence Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cool air associated with a strong upper level low over the Great Lakes region combined with warm Lake Erie waters to produce persistent, intense lake-enhanced rainfall which trained repeatedly over northern Erie County, Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 30th.  Over 4 inches of rain fell along the Interstate 90 corridor from near Girard to just south of Erie, with isolated locations approaching 7 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The persistent line of lake-enhanced showers causing flooding in Erie increased in intensity westward toward Girard after 9am local daylight time, with two to four inches of rain falling in less than two hours.  Earlier rains had fallen over the headwaters of Elk Creek, creating a high water crest that reached Fairview and Girard Townships just as the additional heavy rainfall struck.   Several roads along Elk Creek were flooded, including North Creek Road near Girard, where water levels were the highest seen in over 20 years.  In Fairview Township, floodwaters from Walnut Creek rendered US Highway 20 impassable, while Trout Run inundated areas along Maple Street just north of Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an approaching cold front. Isolated severe storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a large tree and large branches down at two different places along Route 171 between Susquehanna and Hallstead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped trigger an isolated severe thunderstorm across southeastern Pennsylvania during the early evening of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell from a severe thunderstorm in both Brookhaven and Upland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain affected eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening on the 13th and caused flooding. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain upstream caused flooding to continue into the night of the 13th along lower stretches of the Neshaminy Creek. The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne was above its 9 foot flood stage from 717 p.m. EDT on the 13th through 259 a.m. EDT on the 14th. It crested at 11.9 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 14th. Storm totals included 4.61 inches in Richboro and 2.21 inches in Langhorne.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a downburst with winds estimated around 60 mph near Mechanicsburg in Monroe Township. Sporadic tree damage was noted on Williams Grove Road at the West Shore Evangelical Free Church. Tree damage was also noted on Eppley Road between Stoner Road and Fisher Road...extending southward into the Monroe Acres development along Fairway Drive and points east. Several utility poles (3) were also caused to lean from the wind along Williams Grove Road. Numerous roads were flooded and closed in the town, and the Mayor issued a disaster declaration. In addition, over 200 basements were flooded.||The flash flooding continued and transitioned into flooding, which persisted overnight. Of note, Tulpehocken Street was covered with 2 to 3 feet of water. Swatara Creek at Pine Grove was exceeded its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley pushed warm and humid air northward ahead of a cold front moving east with the surface low. Severe thunderstorms were widespread across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Significant flash flooding occurred over eastern Allegheny and western Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania with estimated damage of 18 million dollars to public infrastructure and private buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding over Allegheny county from the eastern sections of downtown Pittsburgh to Monroeville and areas to the Westmoreland county line. 29 people were trapped in cars, and Emergency Medical Services crews rescued 14 of them. 70 to 80 people were evacuated from about 30 homes in the hardest hit Turtle Creek area. 3 to 4 inches of rain fell across the area in about 3 hours. Allegheny county reported 1,658 buildings were damaged due to flash flooding. Most major roadways were flooded with several closed for hours to remove debris. The estimated damage to both public infrastructure and private buildings is estimated between 8 and 10 million dollars.

This caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding along the Wissahickon and Pennypack Creeks. Several roads were closed due to the flooding and marine rescues from stranded vehicles. The worst reported flooding occurred along the Pennypack Creek at the Robert Bruce Apartments along the Hatboro and Upper Moreland Township line. The creek flooded into the parking lot and submerged a few vehicles. Residents moved from some lower lying apartments to higher ones as a precaution. Event precipitation totals included 3.78 inches in Willow Grove and 3.50 inches in Ambler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed and rapidly intensified ahead of an upper level trough moving eastward across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening. The convective environment was characterized by moderate instability supported by surface temperatures in the 80s and dewpoints in the 60s and 70s, and unidirectional westerly wind profiles on the order of 30 to 40 knots. These conditions favored organized storms with the potential for line and bowing segments to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Bellwood in Antis Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first severe thunderstorm of the evening to affect Berks County caused widespread tree damage in the southeast part of the county. Scores of trees were knocked down. The worst reported damage was in Amity Township. About thirty homes were damaged, two were destroyed by fallen trees and resulted in the displacement of those two families. About 80 other people were displaced temporarily. Many trees were knocked down along Levengood Road and County Routes 562 and 662 in the township. Downed trees and poles blocked and closed County Route 562. In Douglas Township, the Manatawny Winery was damaged. A pheasant farm near Yellow House was damaged. Wires were knocked down throughout the township. In Boyertown, a tent was overturned. Numerous trees were also knocked down in Oley and Earl Townships. About 2,800 homes and businesses lost power in the county because of the series of severe thunderstorms. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved across Montgomery and Bucks Counties dropped heavy rain over the region. An unseasonably wet June made it easier for flash flooding to occur across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of streams in the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek basin in Franconia and Salford Townships. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3.6 inches in this area, most of which fell within one hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near New Bloomfield.

Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached three inches in the area and a measured storm total of 2.47 inches was reported from nearby Downingtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain affected eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening on the 13th and caused flooding. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage as well as flash flooding of smaller streams and creeks including the headwaters of the Neshaminy Creek in and around Doylestown, Warwick, Warrington and Westminster Townships in central Bucks County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 5 and 6 inches in the area.  In Warwick Township, two water rescues were performed by emergency responders. A man became stranded in his vehicle after the Neshaminy overflowed on Creek Road and a woman was rescued from her vehicle on Stover Street after a retention basin overflowed. In Doylestown, both Pine Run and Chapman Road were closed. The Little Neshaminy Creek at Neshaminy was above its 8 foot flood stage from 337 p.m. EDT through 939 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 9.21 feet at 445 p.m. EDT. Event storm totals included 4.61 inches in Richboro (Northampton Township).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down numerous trees just off of Route 54 west of Barnesville.

The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 941 p.m. EDT on the 13th through 1239 a.m. EDT on the 14th. It crested at 7.8 feet at 1130 p.m. EDT on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southeast across the Great Lakes triggered severe thunderstorms across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours. These storms produced several reports of damaging winds and large hail ranging in size from penny to half-dollar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and  thunderstorms developed ahead of a frontal boundary that moved east across northeast Pennsylvania in the afternoon. Isolated severe storms developed, with one thunderstorm producing a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey confirmed a tornado touchdown at the Skyhaven Airport. The first signs of damage were to a metal roof which was peeled away from a farm outbuilding. Pieces of this roof were moved in a northeast direction for over 400 yards. There was limited damaged to trees, mainly consisting of small downed branches. Toward the edge of the airport, corn stalks were  blown toward the northeast. On the other side of a grove of pine trees, pieces of the metal roof were blown back toward the west and southwest. Pieces of roof ended up toward the emergency operations center, about 1.21 miles from the building from which it was peeled. The tornado continued northeast to the edge of the runway before lifting up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms brought locally heavy rainfall to parts of northeast Pennsylvania. Minor flooding problems resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were closed due to flooding. One house was surrounded by water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms brought locally heavy rainfall to parts of northeast Pennsylvania. Minor flooding problems resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lee Road Bridge over Dudaff Creek was washed out and Route 106 was closed. Some basement flooding occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system pushed a slow moving cold front across upstate northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon. Scattered thunderstorms developed along and near the front, with isolated storms producing strong winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew six trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and  thunderstorms developed ahead of a frontal boundary that moved east across northeast Pennsylvania in the afternoon. Isolated severe storms developed, with one thunderstorm producing a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down behind a house on Jurista Hill Road. The first signs of damage were to the top quarter of a pine tree which was twisted and bent over the tree itself. Continuing northeast, one large tree was uprooted with one smaller tree snapped. Another large tree was uprooted, and an 8 foot by 16 foot wooden shed rolled over for 30 yards. More tree branches were broken before the tornado moved toward a corn field, damaging a barn. After minor damage to small areas of the corn field, more trees were snapped and uprooted before the tornado lifted. The tornado passed right next to a two story house, with only a small section of the metal roof showing signs of uplifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved through the eastern part of Pennsylvania Friday afternoon helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon of the 31st mainly around the local Philadelphia area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a couple of trees in Penn and London Grove Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching surface trof to produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across Eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous trees and wires in northeastern Bucks County. This forced the closures of many roads including Pennsylvania State Route 611 (Easton Road) in Nockamixon Township and Pennsylvania State Route 32 (River Road) in Tinicum Township. There were about 20 downed trees on Easton Road in Nockamixon Township. In Upper Black Eddy (Bridgeton Township), a downed tree damaged a porch. Roadways were still closed the following day (27th) in Plumstead, Solebury, Bridgeton and Nockamixon Townships. About 3,000 homes and businesses lost power. Peak wind gusts were estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and several instances of isolated flash flooding in Warren County throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding near Warren. Water was covering roads in North Warren, and one house was surrounded by water in Warren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late morning. PECO Energy reported about 54,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power due to the lightning and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The vacant historic Garrett-Dunn House in the Mount Airy section of Philadelphia was struck and destroyed by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late morning. PECO Energy reported about 54,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power due to the lightning and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms downed numerous tree limbs and several trees across the southern or lower part of Bucks County. Broken tree limbs littered several roadways in the county. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 11,000 homes and businesses to lose power.

PECO Energy reported about 54,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power due to the lightning and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms tore down numerous tree limbs and trees mainly across the southeastern half of Chester County.

PECO Energy reported about 54,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power due to the lightning and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees within Philadelphia. The combination of the damaging winds, lightning and heavy rain caused about 11,000 homes and businesses within the city to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late morning. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tore down several trees in Bethel and Concord Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late morning. PECO Energy reported about 54,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power due to the lightning and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees in Yeadon Borough. One downed tree blocked the intersection of Providence Road and Lansdowne Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area with heavy rain and flash flooding. The region was already saturated from all of the heavy rain that fell during the last week of July. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached as high as 4 to 5 inches across eastern Chester, southern Montgomery and southwestern Bucks Counties and caused flash flooding. Doppler Radar storm total estimates also exceeded 3 inches in parts of western and southern Berks County. Because of the flooding, Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell requested and received approval from the United States Small Business Administration to have disaster area declaration for Berks, Bucks, Chester, Delaware, Lehigh, Montgomery and Philadelphia Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flooding of streams in the Octoraro Creek basin in southwestern Chester County. Roadway flooding occurred in Oxford and flooding was reported in Pine Grove (Lower Oxford Township) south of the Octoraro Lake. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 3 inches in the southwest part of Chester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area with heavy rain and flash flooding. The region was already saturated from all of the heavy rain that fell during the last week of July. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached as high as 4 to 5 inches across eastern Chester, southern Montgomery and southwestern Bucks Counties and caused flash flooding. Doppler Radar storm total estimates also exceeded 3 inches in parts of western and southern Berks County. Because of the flooding, Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell requested and received approval from the United States Small Business Administration to have disaster area declaration for Berks, Bucks, Chester, Delaware, Lehigh, Montgomery and Philadelphia Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding of smaller streams and roadway flooding in upper Bucks County. Several roadways were closed. Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 2 to 3 inches. In Springfield Township, Cooks Creek flooded and Durham Road was closed. Other roadways flooded and closed included U.S. Route 309 in Richland and Pennsylvania State Route 313 in Quakertown. Roads were closed in East Rockhill Township. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek was flooding in Perkasie. A couple of roadways suffererd poor drainage flooding along the Delaware River in Solebury, Plumstead and Tinicum Townships. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Dublin was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1235 p.m. EDT through 120 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 5.16 feet at 1245 p.m. EDT. Storm rainfall totals included 3.68 inches in Pennsburg, 3.66 inches in Doylestown, 2.70 inches in Springtown and 1.85 inches in Perkasie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place on the 21st ahead of a cold front. This resulted in the development of some thunderstorms from west to east, some of which were strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning in Penn Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west during the 22nd, while Hurricane Bill passed well to our east. An abundance of moisture in place combined with enough instability, which resulted in showers and thunderstorms with locally torrential rainfall and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Frankford Creek within Philadelphia. The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue was above its 7 foot flood stage from 714 p.m. EDT through 752 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 7.8 feet at 730 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 3.35 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area with heavy rain and flash flooding. The region was already saturated from all of the heavy rain that fell during the last week of July. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached as high as 4 to 5 inches across eastern Chester, southern Montgomery and southwestern Bucks Counties and caused flash flooding. Doppler Radar storm total estimates also exceeded 3 inches in parts of western and southern Berks County. Because of the flooding, Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell requested and received approval from the United States Small Business Administration to have disaster area declaration for Berks, Bucks, Chester, Delaware, Lehigh, Montgomery and Philadelphia Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding along the Neshaminy Creek basin in lower or southern Bucks County.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached three to four inches in the area. The Neshaminy Creek covered roads and backyards near Langhorne. Four people were canoeing along the rising creek and were thrown into it along the Northampton and Wrightstown border. They were rescued. Numerous roads were flooded in Newtown and Warrington Townships. The Little Neshaminy Creek near Neshaminy was above its 8 foot flood stage from 1130 a.m. EDT through 502 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 9.05 feet at Noon EDT. Storm totals included 3.53 inches in Southampton, 3.46 inches in Furlong and 2.46 inches in Langhorne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west during the 22nd, while Hurricane Bill passed well to our east. An abundance of moisture in place combined with enough instability, which resulted in showers and thunderstorms with locally torrential rainfall and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding along some of the smaller streams within Lower Merion Township. In the Wynnewood section of the township, the West Branch of the Indian Creek flooded along Allandale and Andover Roads. Cars in driveways were inundated and damaged or destoryed. A few water rescues were performed and basements were flooded. Mud and debris was left about on third of the way up driveways. Vehicle water rescues were also performed along the Mill Creek.  Precipitation event totals included 5.35 inches in Wynnewood, 3.35 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 2.45 inches in Norristown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary settled southward across most of the area during the 28th before stalling nearby. Plenty of moisture flowing northward along with incoming energy resulted in some showers and thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rainfall and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Little Lehigh and Saucon Creeks in southern Lehigh County. Several roads were flooded and closed. One lane of U.S. Route 222 was also closed in Trexlertown. Other road closures occurred on Spring Creek Road in Lower Macungie Township and Meadows Road in Lower Saucon Township. Spring Creek Road was closed into the evening.  Flooding along the Little Lehigh Creek extended into Allentown near Bogert's Bridge. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 2 and 3 inches in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high pressure on the 18th was off the Mid Atlantic coast, while a cold front was well off to the west. In between these two systems was a surface trough, which helped to focus showers and thunderstorms within a warm and humid air mass. The thunderstorms, some severe, marched across the region during the evening hours. The combination of high winds and lightning caused about 27,500 homes and businesses to lose power in Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania. Power was not fully restored until the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was measured and penny size hail fell in Skippack Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system brought isolated showers and thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon, with isolated flash flooding occurring due to heavy rainfall from slow moving storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms caused a significant urban flood in Swoyersville. Water and debris was several feet deep across Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon, with isolated storms producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, from the late afternoon into the night. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused roadway flooding as well as flooding of smaller streams in northern Lehigh County in and around Heidelberg Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 6 inches in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure was located off the East Coast on the 10th, a hot and very humid air mass (dew points between 70 and 75 degrees was across the area. This resulted in peak heat indices at above the century mark. At the Philadelphia International Airport, the heat index topped out at 103 degrees. The high temperature on the 10th was 95 degrees, which was tied with August 21st as the hottest day of the year.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused roadway flooding as well as flooding of smaller streams in east centra; Lehigh County in and around Whitehall and North Whitehall Townships. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 6 inches in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, from the late afternoon into the night. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain that fell during the night of the 12th caused flooding along sections of the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek in Chester County. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 519 a.m. EDT through 1028 a.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 10.11 feet at 815 a.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary, caused thunderstorms to last into the night of the 12th across Eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike to a home on West White Street in Lehighton shattered its chimney and scattered cement blocks fifty feet around the home. One cement block made a ten inch hole in the home's roof. The lightning strike also damaged the electrical wiring within the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, from the late afternoon into the night. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused significant roadway flooding as well as flooding of smaller streams in and around Lake Harmony in Kidder Township. Roadway flooding and flooding of smaller streams also occurred in the western part of the county. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 5 to 6 inches within the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place on the 21st ahead of a cold front. This resulted in the development of some thunderstorms from west to east, some of which were strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees across central Carbon County from Mahoning Township east through Towamensing Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning in Sadsbury Township. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Washington Township in northeastern Lehigh County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Upper Saucon Township  in southeastern Lehigh County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees in Birdsboro Borough. About 800 homes and businesses lost power. Power was restored by 6 p.m. EDT that evening.

Hardest hit was Haverford Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches in the northeast part of the county. ||In Haverford Township, an emergency disaster was declared. Homes bordering Cobbs Creek and its tributaries were damaged. On Eagle Road, cars were submerged and homes were surrounded by water. Roads and bridges on Hathaway Lane were damaged. Homes and vehicles on Wynnefield Drive were flooded. Six roads in the township were flooded. In Darby Borough, flood waters from the Darby Creek washed away a portion of McDade Boulevard. Ten residents were evacuated from Springfield Road. Roadway and basement flooding also occurred in Lansdowne Borough and Chester City. The precipitation event total at the Philadelphia International Airport was 1.59 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires across Pennsylvania State Route 423 in Coolbaugh Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place on the 21st ahead of a cold front. This resulted in the development of some thunderstorms from west to east, some of which were strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous tree limbs, trees and wires throughout Chester County, mainly along the southern tier. One tree fell on an occupied vehicle in Kenneth Township, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, from the late afternoon into the night. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and flooding of smaller streams in northeastern Montgomery County in and around Green Lane (Marlborough Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure was located off the East Coast on the 10th, a hot and very humid air mass (dew points between 70 and 75 degrees was across the area. This resulted in peak heat indices at above the century mark. At the Philadelphia International Airport, the heat index topped out at 103 degrees. The high temperature on the 10th was 95 degrees, which was tied with August 21st as the hottest day of the year.

An area of high pressure on the 18th was off the Mid Atlantic coast, while a cold front was well off to the west. In between these two systems was a surface trough, which helped to focus showers and thunderstorms within a warm and humid air mass. The thunderstorms, some severe, marched across the region during the evening hours. The combination of high winds and lightning caused about 27,500 homes and businesses to lose power in Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania. Power was not fully restored until the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Tulpehocken Township.

A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area with heavy rain and flash flooding. The region was already saturated from all of the heavy rain that fell during the last week of July. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached as high as 4 to 5 inches across eastern Chester, southern Montgomery and southwestern Bucks Counties and caused flash flooding. Doppler Radar storm total estimates also exceeded 3 inches in parts of western and southern Berks County. Because of the flooding, Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell requested and received approval from the United States Small Business Administration to have disaster area declaration for Berks, Bucks, Chester, Delaware, Lehigh, Montgomery and Philadelphia Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding in the headwaters of the Perkiomen River Basin in northern Montgomery County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged around three inches in the area. The West Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Hillegass was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1213 p.m. EDT through 110 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 5.11 feet at 1245 p.m. EDT. The Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 1142 a.m. EDT through 414 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 4.91 feet at 315 p.m. EDT. In Palm, the actual storm precipitation total was 3.22 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain from the thunderstorms during the morning of the 2nd caused minor flooding along the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown during the afternoon of the 2nd. The creek was above its 7 foot flood stage from 342 p.m. EDT through 436 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 7.09 feet at 415 p.m. EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding of streams as well as poor drainage flooding in southeastern Berks County from around Cumru and Robeson Townships northeast through Rockland and Pike Townships. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached four inches within this area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding along the Saucon Creek in Upper Saucon Township as well as flooding along some of the smaller tributaries of the creek in Coopersburg Borough. North Main Street in Coopersburg was flooded by one of these tributaries and closed.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain earlier on the morning of the 2nd caused minor flooding along the Valley Creek. The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 708 a.m. EDT through 801 a.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 7.5 feet at 730 a.m. EDT. The rainfall total through 7 a.m. EDT that morning at Valley Forge was 1.12 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain from showers and thunderstorms earlier in the morning caused flooding along the Wissahickon Creek. The flooding was exacerbated when thunderstorms with even more heavier rain moved across Montgomery County later in the morning on the second. Please refer to the flash flood entry for Montgomery County for further details. The river gage along the Wissahickon Creek was above its 9 foot flood stage from 826 a.m. EDT through 624 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 12.90 feet at 245 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, helped produce some heavy rain. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms knocked down several trees and wires mainly across the central third of Delaware County. One of the larger electrical outages occurred in Sharon Hill Borough where 2,500 homes and businesses lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved northeast in the morning and a cold front that arrived from the west late in the day on the 2nd resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area with heavy rain and flash flooding. The region was already saturated from all of the heavy rain that fell during the last week of July. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the series of thunderstorms during the first half of the day on the 2nd, espeically in Montgomery County, caused flooding along the Wissahickon Creek within Philadelphia. The Wisshaickon Creek at its mouth with the Schuylkill River was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1102 a.m. EDT through 1121 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 7.93 feet at 615 p.m. EDT. Flooding along the Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge also continued into the evening. The creek was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1129 a.m. EDT through 956 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 11.02 feet at 7 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, helped produce some heavy rain. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and the ensuing fire displaced a family of four from their Meadowcrest Lane home in Amity Township. Lightning struck the corner of the home and then traveled through an air duct. It took four fire companies fifteen minutes to extingusih the blaze, but the house suffered smoke and water damage. The damage was estimated at $30,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, helped produce some heavy rain. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees along extreme western Montgomery County from Pottstown to Upper Providence Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, helped produce some heavy rain. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms knocked down several trees and wires throughout Chester County. In addition, the severe thunderstorm destroyed a corn maze at the Hurricane Hill Farm in West Brandywine Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain caused flash flooding of small streams in eastern Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along a small stream tributary to the Yellow Breaches Creek. The  high water covered a bridge along Hertzler Road in Upper Allen Township. ||The flash flooding transitioned into areal county flooding, which persisted until midnight local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 26th. These thunderstorms preceded a cold front that cleared the state around Midnight EDT on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peal to nickel size hail fell from a severe thunderstorm in Laureldale Borough. Nickel size hail also fell in Ruscombmanor Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain and recent wet weather conditions produced some flash flooding across the southeastern part of the state from mid afternoon into early evening on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding along Frankford Creek within Philadelphia. The Frankford Creek at the Castor Avenue gauge exceeded its 7 foot flood stage from 510 p.m. through 540 p.m EDT on the 31st. It crested at 8.26 feet. The storm total at the Philadelphia International Airport was 1.42 inches, most of which fell within one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved through the eastern part of Pennsylvania Friday afternoon helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon of the 31st mainly around the local Philadelphia area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees in East Whiteland, Easttown and Willistown Townships in eastern Chester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy rain across Berks County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along Pennsylvania State Route 61 between Hamburg and Leesport. A couple of vehicles were stranded in the high water. A couple of basements were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms produced 2 to 4 inches of rain which caused flash flooding in far northern Susquehanna County, Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major flash flood occurred in the Little Meadows area, when 2 to 4 inches of rain fell in less than 2 hours. The heavy rain caused debris to block 3 large drainage pipes, which then caused significant flash flooding. Flooding occurred on the lower part of Cork Hill Road. The water ran down from the upper part of the road into this area. The water then entered a home owner's yard. A small bridge was also washed out on State Route 4013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain and recent wet weather conditions produced some flash flooding across the southeastern part of the state from mid afternoon into early evening on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flooding as well as stream flooding in central and southwest Chester County from around Thorndale in Caln Township southwest into West Fallowfield and Lower Oxford Townships. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached as high as 3 to 4 inches in western Chester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rains that moved across the same parts of the Poconos produced flooding during the afternoon and evening of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell over the same areas caused poor drainage and smaller stream flooding in parts of Polk, Eldred, Penn Forest and Towamensing Townships. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall in a short duration associated with slow moving thunderstorms produced flash flooding across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall of 1-2 inches in a short duration resulted in flash flooding across mainly rural areas in extreme southeastern Lancaster County. A mudslide closed one lane of Route 472 in Colerain Township near Mount Eden Road. Two feet of water was also reported covering Spruce Grove Road in Colerain Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved northwest to southeast across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening, triggering scattered severe thunderstorms which produced isolated hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down numerous trees in New Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved northwest to southeast across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening, triggering scattered severe thunderstorms which produced isolated hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Hanover. One tree fell onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved northwest to southeast across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening, triggering scattered severe thunderstorms which produced isolated hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Auburn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved northwest to southeast across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening, triggering scattered severe thunderstorms which produced isolated hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced downburst wind damage over a small area near Hepburna. Winds were generally estimated around 60 mph...but a small area of damage indicated peak winds near 80 mph. About 30 trees were either knocked down or snapped off. One tree fell on a house and caused minor damage to the siding and fascia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 26th. These thunderstorms preceded a cold front that cleared the state around Midnight EDT on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Upper Pottsgrove Township and penny size hail fell in Lower Pottsgrove Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain caused flash flooding of small streams in eastern Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along a small stream tributary of the Yellow Breaches Creek. The high water covered a bridge along Hertzler Road in Upper Allen Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved northwest to southeast across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening, triggering scattered severe thunderstorms which produced isolated hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail accumulated 2 to 3 inches near Hepburna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 26th. These thunderstorms preceded a cold front that cleared the state around Midnight EDT on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees in Pottstown. Trees were knocked down on Center Avenue, Rosedale Drive and Henry Street. Another tree was struck by lightning and fell down on King Street. A large maple tree fell down onto and damaged the porch of a home on Rosedale Drive. In Stowe (Upper Pottsgrove Township), a tree fell onto a roof of a home. About 740 homes and businesses lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 26th. These thunderstorms preceded a cold front that cleared the state around Midnight EDT on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a couple of trees and wires in Coolbaugh and Tobyhanna Townships in northwestern Monroe County. The same thunderstorm dropped small hail (dime size) in Jackson and Pocono Townships. The dime size hail covered the ground in Scotrun (Pocono Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second cold frontal passage in as many days helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Lansdale Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage across central Pennsylvania from the mid afternoon through the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph uprooted large trees and knocked down numerous wires in and around the city of Lancaster. A few trees fell onto cars and damaged surrounding structures. Several thousand customers lost power in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rains that moved across the same parts of the Poconos produced flooding during the afternoon and evening of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell over the same areas caused poor drainage and smaller stream flooding in west central Monroe County with the heaviest rain falling in Polk and Chestnut Townships.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 6 inches. An actual storm total included 2.87 inches in Kresgeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front retreating to the north and a cold front approaching from the west produced strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 29th. One EF-2 tornado also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado with estimated maximum wind speeds of 120 mph touched down and affected Hamilton and Stroud Townships. The highest wind speeds and worst reported damage occurred on Middle Road in Hamilton Township and Stroudsmoor Road in Stroud Township. Two men were injured and about 10,000 homes and businesses lost power. Hundreds of trees were destroyed and at least 25 telephone poles had to be replaced. Power was not fully restored until the 31st. In Hamilton Township, two homes suffered roof damage and four large farm buildings and one garage were destroyed. In Stroud Township, one home lost its roof, two others had trees on their roofs and a section of a resort building lost its roof. This was the first reported tornado in Monroe County since July 1, 2001 and the first reported EF2 or F2 tornado in the county since October 5, 1979.||The tornado touched down southwest of Kemmertown Road in Hamilton Township before causing substantial damage to the Blakeslee Farm near the intersection of Middle and Blakeslee Roads. On Kemmertown Road, a downed tree damaged a porch at an assisted living facility. At the Blakeslee Farm, the tornado destroyed three barns and a garage shed and took the roof and attic off of the home. A 46-year-old man suffered facial and rib injuries and a 66-year-old man suffered a head injury as the garage shed collapsed. In addition to the property damage, the tornado damaged the corn, hay, wheat and oat crops on the farm.   Thirty percent of the trees on the property were destroyed . ||The tornado then lifted, causing only relatively minor damage as it followed Middle Road into Stroud Township. However, more substantial damage again occurred from Larsens Lane northeast to Fox Run Road and Essig Lane.  More damage occurred as the tornado crossed Stroudsmoor Road, where numerous trees were felled, and roof damage occurred to the Stroudsmoor Country Inn. A downed tree also damaged a home on Stroudsmoor Road. Another downed tree damaged a home on Pennsylvania State Route 191. The tornado lifted just before it moved over the intersections of Routes 191 and 611 (Foxtwon Hill Road) just south of Stroudsburg. The tornado was on the ground for about 4.6 miles and its maximum width was about 100 yards. Damage was estimated at 1 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed as a low pressure system moved slowly east from the Great Lakes toward New England. Several storms became severe, producing mainly reports of large hail.

Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed as a low pressure system moved slowly east from the Great Lakes toward New England. Several storms became severe, producing mainly reports of large hail.

Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed as a low pressure system moved slowly east from the Great Lakes toward New England. Several storms became severe, producing mainly reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed as a low pressure system moved slowly east from the Great Lakes toward New England. Several storms became severe, producing mainly reports of large hail.

Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed as a low pressure system moved slowly east from the Great Lakes toward New England. Several storms became severe, producing mainly reports of large hail.

An upper level low pressure system tracking east over the Great Lakes caused  scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop over northeast Pennsylvania. Some of the storms produced small hail, with only isolated storms reaching severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail covered roads near Greeley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon, with isolated storms producing large hail and damaging winds.

A warm front retreating to the north and a cold front approaching from the west produced strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 29th. One EF-2 tornado also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of large trees in Royersford Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell during the late afternoon and early evening on the 24th.  Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches across southeastern Pennsylvania with the heaviest rain across the fall line region from eastern Chester County northeast across southern or lower Montgomery and Bucks Counties. This caused flash flooding during the early evening and runoff flooding through the night of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain earlier in the evening in both Philadelphia and Montgomery Counties caused flooding along the Wissahickon Creek. The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth with the Schuylkill River was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1151 p.m. EDT on the 24th through 449 a.m. EDT on the 25th. It crested at 5.75 feet at 245 a.m. EDT on the 25th.  Event precipitation totals included 1.61 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 1.52 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.50 inches within Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds occurred across Eastern Pennsylvania from the middle of the morning into the early evening on the 7th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and knocked down some weak trees, tree limbs and wires. Power outages were reported in the Poconos in Palmerton (Carbon County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County).  Downed trees were reported in Lehigh County in Allentown, Alburtis and Slatington. In Delaware County in Newtown Square, a downed wire started a small fire.  ||Peak wind gusts included 52 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 47 mph in Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 46 mph in Forks Township (Northampton County), 45 mph in Reading (Berks County) and Coatesville (Chester County), 44 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 43 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County). | |The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system (around 990 millibars) that was moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system moving from the Central Plains toward the area. As the high pressure system moved closer, winds diminished during the evening of the 7th.

In Delaware County in Newtown Square, a downed wire started a small fire.  ||Peak wind gusts included 52 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 47 mph in Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 46 mph in Forks Township (Northampton County), 45 mph in Reading (Berks County) and Coatesville (Chester County), 44 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 43 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County). | |The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system (around 990 millibars) that was moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system moving from the Central Plains toward the area. As the high pressure system moved closer, winds diminished during the evening of the 7th.

An early season heavy wet snowfall wreaked havoc across portions of northern and central Pennsylvania from the mid morning on the 15th through the late morning on the 16th. The combination of heavy wet snow and leaves on the trees made this an extremely destructive and high impact event. Most of the damage was related to large tree limbs and branches falling on homes and power lines. It is estimated that about 27,000 customers lost power. For many locations, this was the earliest snowfall ever recorded in the month of October. Snowfall totals generally ranged between 4 and 6 inches with up to 9 inches on the highest ridge-top elevations. The heaviest snow fell in a swath from central Centre County northward through Northern Clinton into Potter and Tioga Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of heavy wet snow caused extensive damage countywide related to leaf-laden trees and limbs falling on power lines. Upwards of six to eight inches of snow fell across the highest elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell during the late afternoon and early evening on the 24th.  Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches across southeastern Pennsylvania with the heaviest rain across the fall line region from eastern Chester County northeast across southern or lower Montgomery and Bucks Counties. This caused flash flooding during the early evening and runoff flooding through the night of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Schuylkill Expressway and other low lying roadways in Lower Merion Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell during the late afternoon and early evening on the 24th.  Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches across southeastern Pennsylvania with the heaviest rain across the fall line region from eastern Chester County northeast across southern or lower Montgomery and Bucks Counties. This caused flash flooding during the early evening and runoff flooding through the night of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavier rain earlier on the evening of the 24th caused flooding along sections of the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek near Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 859 p.m. EDT on the 24th through 336 a.m. EDT on the 25th. It crested at 8.94 feet at Midnight EDT on the 25th. Event precipitation totals in Montgomery County included 2.68 inches in Willow Grove, 2.57 inches in Hatfield, 2.16 inches in Souderton and 2.14 inches in Graterford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell during the late afternoon and early evening on the 24th.
